name: SLU-PP-332
short_description: SLU-PP-332 is a synthetic small-molecule pan-agonist of estrogen-related receptors (ERRα, ERRβ, ERRγ), developed at Saint Louis University. It is NOT a peptide — it is a low-molecular-weight non-steroidal compound (C18H14N2O2, MW 290.3 g/mol). It functions as an exercise mimetic by transcriptionally activating the aerobic-exercise gene program, enhancing mitochondrial biogenesis, fatty acid oxidation, and oxidative muscle fiber development. All efficacy and safety data as of mid-2026 are from animal (mouse) and in vitro models; no human clinical trials have been registered or completed. It is NOT FDA-approved, has no IND application, and is prohibited by WADA under S4 (Hormone and Metabolic Modulators). Research-only compound. This entry is educational only and does not constitute a dosing recommendation; discuss any compound with a qualified clinician.
mechanism: SLU-PP-332 directly binds and activates all three estrogen-related receptor isoforms (ERRα, ERRβ, ERRγ), with highest potency at ERRα (EC50 = 98 nM). ERRs are orphan nuclear receptors with no identified endogenous hormonal ligand; they act as constitutive transcription factors that regulate energy metabolism. By acting as a pan-ERR agonist, SLU-PP-332 transcriptionally upregulates a broad program of metabolic genes including PGC-1α, TFAM, NRF-1, PDK4, and genes involved in fatty acid β-oxidation, oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS complexes I-IV), and mitochondrial biogenesis. In skeletal muscle, this shifts fiber composition toward oxidative Type IIa fibers and enhances mitochondrial respiratory capacity — effects that phenocopy acute aerobic exercise at the transcriptional level. In cardiac tissue, ERRγ-mediated activation restores fatty acid metabolism and contractile function in pressure-overload models. The compound does not bind classical estrogen receptors (ERα/ERβ) and is structurally non-steroidal, minimizing direct estrogenic signaling.
monitoring_notes: This compound has no established human monitoring protocol — the following are precautionary, clinician-discretion considerations, not directives
Baseline and periodic cardiac evaluation (ECG, echocardiogram) may be appropriate in any research context given ERR cardiac activity

Liver function tests (ALT, AST) — no hepatotoxicity was seen in mouse studies at published doses, but human hepatic metabolism produces 22+ metabolites (per Avliyakulov et al., Drug Testing and Analysis, 2026)
Baseline cancer screening may be appropriate before any experimental ERR-agonist use given ERRα's role in tumor biology
Monitor for cardiovascular symptoms: palpitations, chest discomfort, dyspnea
This compound is research-only; any clinical use requires IRB oversight and appropriate institutional research-committee approval
absolute_contraindications: Personal or family history of hormone-sensitive cancers (breast, ovarian, endometrial, prostate) — unresolved mechanistic concern from ERRα's role in tumor metabolic support
Pregnancy and lactation — ERR signaling regulates placental and reproductive biology; no safety data
Active participation in WADA-governed sport — compound is prohibited under S4
relative_contraindications: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy or other structural heart disease — ERR activation alters cardiac metabolism; effects in diseased human hearts are unknown
Pre-existing metabolic or endocrine disorders on active pharmacotherapy — potential pharmacodynamic interactions unstudied
Any cancer history — theoretical ERRα tumor-promotion concern; no human data to quantify risk
Immunosuppressed individuals — ERRs regulate immune cell metabolism; effects unstudied
common_side_effects: Increased heart rate (anecdotally reported in community contexts, plausibly reflecting broad metabolic activation; not formally characterized in controlled studies)
Injection-site reactions (associated with subcutaneous administration of reconstituted compound)
Elevated body temperature / thermogenesis (an expected pharmacodynamic effect of increased energy expenditure)
Fatigue or lethargy at higher exposures (anecdotal; not documented in published animal studies)
serious_adverse_events: Unknown long-term cardiovascular effects — ERRα/γ activation drives cardiac metabolic gene programs; cardiac hypertrophy has been observed in some murine ERR-activation contexts (mechanistic concern, not confirmed with SLU-PP-332 specifically at published doses)

Potential oncologic risk: ERRα is overexpressed in multiple tumor types (breast, ovarian, prostate, colorectal) and associated with worse prognosis; whether agonist activation promotes or suppresses tumor growth in established cancers is unresolved and context-dependent (mechanistic concern, no human data)
Unknown immunologic and endocrine effects from chronic pan-ERR activation in humans — ERRs regulate immune metabolism and reproductive biology
No human pharmacovigilance data exist; the full adverse-event profile is unknown
WADA S4 prohibited substance — a positive doping test is a certain consequence for competitive athletes

(Frontiers in Physiology, 2025, pilot study): SLU-PP-332 at 1×10⁻⁵ M working concentration applied to cultured human myoblasts derived from biopsies of inactive elderly women. Produced 37.7% reduction in ROS, 117.4% increase in glutathione, 26.1% decrease in senescence markers, and upregulation of SIRT1/PGC-1α/ERRα/FNDC5. Non-toxic up to 1×10⁻⁴ M in cell culture. This is ex-vivo cell-culture data, NOT in-vivo human administration. Dose is a molar culture concentration, not a body dose; numeric mg/mcg dose values are intentionally nulled because no validated human in-vivo dose exists. Not a dosing recommendation.

dose_references.notes: Community/research-practice convention, not clinically validated. This range (0.5–1.5 mg/day SC) is widely cited across research-community sites and reflects rough allometric scaling from published mouse doses (50 mg/kg IP), not any validated human pharmacokinetic or clinical-trial data. SLU-PP-332 has NOT been tested in human subjects; oral bioavailability in humans is uncharacterized (a successor compound SLU-PP-915 was developed explicitly because SLU-PP-332 'lacks oral bioavailability'). This row reflects community/research-practice convention ONLY and is NOT clinically validated, NOT a recommendation, and must be displayed only as a reference range with a mandatory clinician disclaimer.

synergies.rationale: Mechanistically complementary at the educational level: SLU-PP-332 activates ERR-mediated transcriptional exercise programs while MOTS-c engages AMPK and mitochondrial signaling. Both are described in the literature as enhancing mitochondrial function and fatty acid metabolism via distinct upstream pathways. No co-administration studies in animals or humans have been published as of 2026; any combined use is unstudied.
synergies.caution_notes: Additive cardiovascular stress from two simultaneous metabolic activators is unstudied. No human safety data exist for either compound in isolation, and combination data are entirely absent. Educational framing only — not a protocol or recommendation.

synergies.rationale: At a mechanistic level, ERR activation and NAD+/sirtuin signaling both converge on PGC-1α co-activation and mitochondrial biogenesis (SIRT1 deacetylates PGC-1α; SIRT1 activity depends on NAD+). This convergence is frequently discussed in longevity biology. No animal or human studies combining SLU-PP-332 with NMN have been published.
synergies.caution_notes: Theoretical convergence only. NMN human data exist but not in combination with SLU-PP-332. Potential for compounded cardiovascular/metabolic load is unstudied. Educational framing only.

synergies.rationale: AOD-9604 (a C-terminal fragment of human growth hormone) is described as promoting lipolysis, while SLU-PP-332 is described as enhancing oxidative/β-oxidation machinery. Conceptually these target different nodes (fat mobilization vs. oxidative utilization). This is a theoretical rationale only; no co-administration studies exist.
synergies.caution_notes: Speculative pairing. Both compounds lack validated human safety profiles as standalone agents; combination risk is unknown. Not a studied protocol and not a recommendation.

name: ACE-031 (ActRIIB-Fc / Ramatercept)
short_description: ACE-031 (also called Ramatercept) is a recombinant fusion protein — NOT a classic peptide — consisting of the extracellular domain of human activin receptor type IIB (ActRIIB) fused to the Fc region of human IgG1. It acts as a soluble decoy receptor that competitively sequesters myostatin and related TGF-beta superfamily ligands (activin A/B, GDF-11, BMP9, BMP10), thereby removing the inhibitory brake on skeletal muscle growth. Development was permanently discontinued in 2013 by Acceleron Pharma and Shire due to vascular adverse events (epistaxis, gingival bleeding, telangiectasias) caused by off-target inhibition of BMP9/BMP10, ligands critical for vascular homeostasis. ACE-031 is NOT FDA-approved, has no approved therapeutic indication, is classified as a research chemical, and is prohibited by WADA (S4.3, Hormone and Metabolic Modulators). Black market products have been shown by 2025 anti-doping research to contain incorrect or impure material. Any human self-administration is unapproved, unvalidated, and potentially dangerous. This entry is educational only and does not constitute a dose recommendation.
mechanism: ACE-031 functions as a soluble ligand trap. The extracellular domain of ActRIIB binds circulating myostatin (GDF-8), GDF-11, activin A, activin B, and certain bone morphogenetic proteins (including BMP9 and BMP10) with high affinity, preventing their engagement with native ActRIIB on muscle cell surfaces. Blocking myostatin-ActRIIB binding suppresses downstream Smad2/3 and MAPK signaling that normally inhibits myoblast proliferation and differentiation, releasing the inhibitory constraint on skeletal muscle hypertrophy. The Fc fusion confers a long half-life (~10-15 days in humans) enabling biweekly to monthly dosing in trial settings. The inherent non-selectivity of the receptor-based trap approach (vs. a myostatin-specific antibody) is the mechanistic source of its vascular toxicity: sequestration of BMP9 and BMP10 disrupts endothelial cell maintenance, causing telangiectasias and bleeding. In metabolic contexts, myostatin also suppresses adipogenesis pathways, and ACE-031 produced trends toward reduced fat mass and improved bone mineral density in clinical trials, consistent with myostatin's pleiotropic roles.
monitoring_notes: Dermatological monitoring: regular skin inspection for telangiectasias (the sentinel adverse event that halted clinical development)
ENT / mucosal bleeding surveillance: monitor for epistaxis and gingival bleeding at each follow-up
CBC and coagulation panel: baseline and periodic monitoring given vascular/bleeding signal
Cardiovascular assessment: baseline ECG and blood pressure; myostatin plays a role in cardiac muscle homeostasis and the long-term cardiac effects of ActRIIB inhibition are not characterized
Hormonal panel (females): FSH and reproductive hormone monitoring — activin inhibition was shown to reduce FSH levels
Liver function tests: Fc-fusion protein pharmacokinetics may be affected by hepatic clearance; no hepatic safety data from terminated trials
Antibody / immunogenicity testing: as a fusion protein administered repeatedly, anti-drug antibody formation is possible and was not fully characterized before program termination

NOTE: These monitoring points are extrapolated from the clinical trial adverse event profile. No approved monitoring protocol exists for ACE-031 because it is not an approved drug. All use requires physician oversight.
absolute_contraindications: Any known vascular disorder or hereditary hemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT) — mechanism directly implicates the BMP9/BMP10/endoglin pathway relevant to HHT
Pregnancy or planned pregnancy — activin signaling (inhibited by ACE-031) is essential for reproductive and fetal development
Active or recent significant bleeding disorders or anticoagulant therapy — additive hemorrhagic risk
Competitive athletes subject to WADA or equivalent anti-doping rules — ACE-031 is prohibited under S4.3
Use of any black market or unverified research chemical formulation — product contamination documented in 2025 peer-reviewed anti-doping literature
relative_contraindications: Pre-existing cardiovascular disease or uncontrolled hypertension — vascular safety profile not characterized

Active or history of cancer — TGF-beta superfamily signaling has complex tumor-suppressive and pro-tumorigenic roles; broad inhibition via ActRIIB trap has not been studied for safety in oncology patients
Female patients with reproductive concerns — clinically significant FSH suppression was observed, with unclear fertility implications
Pediatric patients outside of a clinical trial context — the only pediatric data come from terminated DMD trials with significant adverse event rates
Concurrent use of other unapproved anabolic or GH-axis compounds — drug interaction profiles completely unstudied

Pre-existing tendinopathy or connective-tissue disorder: disproportionate muscle-mass gain without commensurate tendon adaptation is a theoretical mechanical-mismatch risk of myostatin/ActRIIB inhibition; unstudied in humans.
common_side_effects: Injection-site erythema (most common in Phase 1)
Epistaxis (nosebleeds) — observed in Phase 2, prompted trial halt
Gingival bleeding (gum bleeding) — observed in Phase 2
Telangiectasias (dilated superficial blood vessels visible on skin) — observed in Phase 2, vascular signal
Decrease in serum FSH levels (females) — pharmacodynamic off-target effect via activin inhibition
serious_adverse_events: Telangiectasias and mucocutaneous bleeding: attributed to off-target inhibition of BMP9 and BMP10 via ActRIIB, critical regulators of endothelial cell homeostasis and vascular integrity. These vascular events caused Acceleron Pharma to halt clinical development in 2011 and permanently discontinue the program in 2013.
39% treatment-related adverse event rate in the Phase 2 DMD trial (NCT01099761) — substantially higher than placebo arms.
Potential for unknown long-term cardiovascular remodeling consequences from broad TGF-beta superfamily suppression — not characterized because trials were terminated.

Black market ACE-031 products (2025 analysis, PMID 40312924): 2 of 14 tested products contained no active protein; all 12 that did contained contaminating proteins and had incorrect molecular structure (full-length ActRIIB rather than the Fc-fusion). Unknown impurities pose uncharacterized immunogenic and toxicological risks.
WADA prohibited substance (S4.3): use in competitive athletes constitutes a doping violation.

Primary adverse event was injection-site erythema; vascular events not yet observed at the single-dose level. These doses were used in a controlled, supervised research setting for a now-discontinued program and do NOT represent a therapeutic dose for any approved indication. Educational reference only — not a dose recommendation.

citation.title: Myostatin inhibitor ACE-031 treatment of ambulatory boys with Duchenne muscular dystrophy: Results of a randomized, placebo-controlled clinical trial

dose_references.notes: Campbell C et al., Muscle Nerve 2017, PMID 27462804. Randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled ascending-dose trial in DMD boys. Doses up to 2.5 mg/kg SC q2-4 weeks. The trial was STOPPED after the second dosing regimen due to epistaxis, gingival bleeding, and telangiectasias attributable to BMP9/BMP10 inhibition; because of this early termination the efficacy data are underpowered and not confirmatory. Trends toward lean mass gain (+4-5%), improved 6-min walk distance (not statistically significant), reduced fat mass, and increased bone mineral density were observed but not confirmed. This dose range led to program discontinuation and must NOT be interpreted as a safe or therapeutic dose. Educational reference only — not a dose recommendation.

citation.title: Gel Electrophoretic Detection of Black Market ACE-031

dose_references.notes: This row reflects a community/research-practice convention, not clinically validated. It is the range most commonly cited in online peptide and research-practice communities for ACE-031 self-administration, extrapolated loosely from the low-end arms of Phase 1 clinical data. It does NOT reflect any approved indication, does NOT account for individual variation, and does NOT account for product purity (black market products have been shown to contain incorrect or impure material; Reichel et al., Drug Test Analysis 2025, PMID 40312924) or the vascular safety signals that caused permanent program discontinuation. Presented for educational reference only — community/research-practice convention, not clinically validated. Clinician guidance and medical supervision are mandatory before any use.

synergies.rationale: Mechanistically, myostatin inhibition removes the inhibitory ceiling on muscle hypertrophy while resistance exercise provides the anabolic stimulus (mTOR activation, satellite cell recruitment) that drives fiber remodeling. Preclinical and early clinical data are consistent with the combination producing greater lean mass accrual than either input alone. The Campbell et al. 2017 DMD trial included physical therapy, and the Phase 1 healthy-volunteer study context involved exercise-induced hypertrophic signaling.
synergies.caution_notes: EDUCATIONAL NOTE ONLY. ACE-031 is not approved and development was permanently discontinued. No human trial formally tested exercise co-intervention as a primary variable. Resistance training is the safe, evidence-based component of this pairing.

synergies.rationale: Myostatin and IGF-1/Akt/mTOR signaling operate on partially non-overlapping anabolic pathways. Mechanistic cell-culture evidence shows IGF-1 can rescue Akt activation suppressed by myostatin, which is consistent with additive hypertrophy signaling when the inhibitory (myostatin) brake is removed and the anabolic (IGF-1) pathway is engaged. A Relative Contributions review (PMC6221906) frames these pathways as mechanistically distinct contributors to body composition.
synergies.caution_notes: No human combination trial exists for ACE-031 + IGF-1. Both are unapproved for this use. Additive off-target risks (vascular, metabolic, neoplastic) are unstudied. EDUCATIONAL context only — not a recommendation to combine.

synergies.rationale: Follistatin is an endogenous glycoprotein that binds and neutralizes myostatin, activin A, and other TGF-beta family members at overlapping but not identical epitopes compared with ActRIIB-Fc. Preclinical mouse data (PMC6204036) describe engineered follistatin producing dual myostatin + activin A antagonism with greater muscle hypertrophy and less fibrosis than myostatin blockade alone, which is consistent with an additive pathway at the mechanistic level.
synergies.caution_notes: Follistatin-344 is also unapproved for human use, and combining two broad ActRII-axis ligand traps would be expected to amplify BMP9/BMP10 inhibition and the vascular toxicity that halted ACE-031 development. This pairing carries serious uncharacterized risk and is presented for mechanistic education only — not a recommendation to stack.

synergies.rationale: Creatine supplementation has been associated in resistance-trained rats with reduced serum myostatin levels and increased expression of myostatin-negative regulatory genes (PMC10181225). In vitro data describe creatine partially counteracting myostatin-induced muscle atrophy. At the mechanistic level, an endogenous myostatin-modulating supplement and a pharmacological myostatin trap act on related biology, though no co-administration human trial exists.
synergies.caution_notes: Creatine is safe and well-studied on its own. The combination with ACE-031 specifically has not been trialed in humans, and creatine's mild endogenous myostatin-lowering effect does not replicate ACE-031's pharmacological mechanism or its risks. EDUCATIONAL framing only.

name: AICAR (Acadesine / AICA Riboside)
short_description: AICAR (acadesine) is a nucleoside analog and AMPK activator — not a peptide, but a small-molecule purine intermediate. It is NOT FDA-approved for any indication. It is classified as a research compound and is banned by WADA (since 2009) due to exercise-mimetic effects demonstrated in animal models. Human clinical trials examined it for cardiac surgery protection and oncology; no therapeutic indication was established. Community use as a metabolic and body-recomposition agent is unsupported by human clinical evidence and carries meaningful safety risks.
mechanism: AICAR is a cell-permeable nucleoside that is taken up via adenosine transporters (ENT1, ENT2, CNT2, CNT3) and phosphorylated intracellularly by adenosine kinase to form ZMP (AICA ribonucleotide-5'-monophosphate). ZMP is a structural analog of AMP and allosterically activates AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) — the master cellular energy sensor — by binding to the AMPK gamma subunit. AMPK activation drives: (1) increased GLUT4-mediated glucose uptake in skeletal muscle; (2) enhanced fatty acid oxidation via ACC inhibition; (3) suppression of hepatic gluconeogenesis; (4) mitochondrial biogenesis via PGC-1alpha; and (5) mTORC1 inhibition (anti-anabolic at high activation levels). However, AICAR accumulates in millimolar concentrations and exerts extensive AMPK-independent effects, including inhibition of de novo purine and pyrimidine synthesis, modulation of glycogen phosphorylase and fructose-1,6-bisphosphatase, and activation of checkpoint kinase 1 (Chk1). The primary metabolite is uric acid, which accounts for the compound's dose-dependent hyperuricemia risk. Oral bioavailability is poor (estimated below 5%); human clinical trials have used continuous intravenous infusion.
monitoring_notes: Serum uric acid: baseline and periodically during any research use; AICAR is metabolized to uric acid and dose-dependent hyperuricemia is well-documented in human IV trials
Serum creatinine and eGFR: baseline and periodically; renal toxicity led to trial termination in the MDS/AML study at oncologic doses
Fasting glucose and HbA1c: baseline recommended, especially in individuals with diabetes or metabolic syndrome; AICAR drives insulin-independent glucose uptake
Complete blood count: anemia and thrombocytopenia were observed in oncology IV trials; relevance at lower doses is unknown but a baseline is prudent
Liver function tests (ALT, AST): routine metabolic monitoring; no specific hepatotoxicity signal in available data but prudent given off-target metabolic effects
Blood pressure: infusion-related hypotension observed in clinical trials; monitor in individuals with baseline hypotension or on antihypertensives

IMPORTANT: AICAR is NOT FDA-approved. Any monitoring framework here reflects clinical-trial practice from oncology and cardiac-surgery studies conducted at IV doses far exceeding community ranges. No validated monitoring protocol exists for the subcutaneous community range. Any monitoring should be directed by a qualified clinician.
absolute_contraindications: Active gout or severe hyperuricemia (AICAR is metabolized primarily to uric acid and will exacerbate urate burden)
Significant renal impairment (eGFR below 30 mL/min/1.73m2; renal toxicity observed in clinical trials at high doses; uric acid excretion impaired)
Concomitant use of insulin or sulfonylureas without medical supervision (additive hypoglycemia risk)
Competitive athletes subject to WADA-compliant anti-doping testing (prohibited substance since 2009)
AICAR is NOT FDA-approved — use outside a registered clinical trial or institutional research protocol is use of a non-approved investigational compound
relative_contraindications: Pre-existing hyperuricemia or history of uric acid nephrolithiasis
Moderate renal impairment (eGFR 30–59 mL/min/1.73m2) — increased urate burden and reduced clearance
Type 2 diabetes managed with glucose-lowering medications — AMPK-driven glucose uptake may cause unpredictable glycemic effects
History of cardiac arrhythmia — AMPK activation has complex cardiac electrophysiology effects
Active or recent hematologic malignancy — unsupervised use is inappropriate even though oncology research has explored AICAR as a cytotoxic agent
Concurrent use of other AMPK activators (metformin, berberine, high-dose resveratrol) — additive AMPK activation effects in combination are unstudied
common_side_effects: Hyperuricemia (dose-dependent; documented in human IV trials; managed with allopurinol prophylaxis in oncology studies)
Transient hypoglycemia (AMPK-mediated insulin-independent glucose uptake into skeletal muscle)
Injection-site reactions (reported with subcutaneous community use; no formal human SC data)
Transient anemia and thrombocytopenia (observed at oncologic IV doses; relevance at lower ranges unknown)
Fatigue or malaise (reported anecdotally; plausible given AMPK-mediated mTOR suppression)
serious_adverse_events: Renal impairment / acute kidney injury: a Phase I/II MDS/AML trial (NCT01813838) was terminated due to serious renal toxicities at high IV doses; risk at community doses is unknown, but the primary metabolite is uric acid, a known nephrotoxin at elevated levels
Dose-limiting hyperuricemia: observed at 315 mg/kg IV in the CLL trial, above the established MTD
Infusion-related hypotension: observed in 3 patients during IV infusion in the CLL Phase I/II trial
Severe hypoglycemia: risk in individuals on insulin, sulfonylureas, or other glucose-lowering agents given AICAR's insulin-independent glucose uptake mechanism
WADA prohibition: AICAR is banned in competitive sport under the Hormone and Metabolic Modulators category (since 2009); athletes face disqualification

dose_references.notes: Open-label, single-arm, non-randomized multicenter Phase I/II dose-finding study, n=24 relapsed/refractory CLL patients (downgraded from HUMAN_RCT to HUMAN_OBS because it is an uncontrolled open-label early-phase trial, not a randomized controlled trial). MTD established at 210 mg/kg IV; 315 mg/kg caused dose-limiting toxicities in 2/3 patients. Adverse events included grade >=2 hyperuricemia (managed with prophylactic allopurinol), transient anemia, thrombocytopenia, infusion-related hypotension, and renal impairment in 4 patients (all resolved). Anti-leukemic activity trends observed but the study was not powered for efficacy. This was an oncology context — doses are NOT applicable to body-recomposition use.

dose_references.notes: Murine study (intraperitoneal injection). AICAR alone (500 mg/kg/day x 4 weeks) enhanced running endurance by approximately 44% in sedentary mice. AICAR + GW501516 (PPARdelta agonist) produced synergistic endurance gains. These animal doses are NOT translatable to human use; allometric scaling and bioavailability differences preclude direct extrapolation. The performance-enhancing findings led to WADA prohibition of AICAR in competitive sport.

citation.title: AICAr, a Widely Used AMPK Activator with Important AMPK-Independent Effects: A Systematic Review

dose_references.notes: This range (10–50 mg/kg or roughly 50–150 mg flat dose) reflects community/research-practice convention, not clinically validated. It circulates in biohacking, bodybuilding, and research-peptide forums and secondary review sites. It is NOT derived from a human clinical trial, NOT validated for safety or efficacy in any peer-reviewed study at these doses via the subcutaneous route, and NOT endorsed by any regulatory body. AICAR is not FDA-approved. Community SC use assumes subcutaneous bioavailability comparable to IV; this assumption is unsupported. The primary metabolite is uric acid. Included solely as a reference range for educational cataloging and must be presented with a full clinician disclaimer; it is not a recommendation.

synergies.rationale: Both MOTS-c (a mitochondria-derived peptide) and AICAR are described in the literature as AMPK activators acting through distinct upstream pathways: MOTS-c engages the folate cycle and activates AMPK via AICAR-independent mechanisms, whereas AICAR delivers AMP mimicry directly via ZMP. Mechanistically the two converge on overlapping downstream targets (PGC-1alpha, glucose uptake, fatty acid oxidation). Some secondary review literature discusses additive or synergistic metabolic effects; no human RCT evidence exists for the combination. Educational mechanistic context only — not a recommendation to combine.
synergies.caution_notes: Additive AMPK activation could amplify hypoglycemia risk, especially in individuals on insulin or sulfonylureas. Dual AMPK activation may also suppress mTORC1 more potently, which could impair muscle protein synthesis. No human safety data for the combination. Discuss with a qualified clinician before considering any combination.

synergies.rationale: NAD+ is required for sirtuin (SIRT1/SIRT3) activity, and SIRT1 deacetylates and activates PGC-1alpha — a downstream effector of AMPK signaling. Mechanistically, AMPK activation and NAD+-driven sirtuin activation are described in longevity-pathway literature as converging on shared targets including mitochondrial biogenesis and fatty acid oxidation (the AMPK-SIRT1 axis). This is educational mechanistic context; no human data support this combination for body recomposition.
synergies.caution_notes: Purely mechanistic rationale. Interactions in humans are unstudied and there are no human safety data for this combination. Discuss with a qualified clinician before considering any combination.

synergies.rationale: Metformin indirectly raises AMPK activity by inhibiting mitochondrial Complex I and increasing the cellular AMP:ATP ratio, while AICAR activates AMPK directly via ZMP without altering actual cellular energy status. Both are described as suppressing hepatic gluconeogenesis and improving insulin sensitivity via overlapping AMPK-dependent mechanisms; this overlap has been characterized in preclinical metabolic models. Educational mechanistic context only.
synergies.caution_notes: Combining two AMPK activators raises the risk of additive or synergistic hypoglycemia, compounds metformin's lactic-acidosis risk in renal impairment, and may excessively suppress mTOR. AICAR is not FDA-approved and this combination has no human safety data. Anyone taking metformin should consult their physician before considering AICAR.

name: FOXO4-DRI
short_description: FOXO4-DRI is a synthetic all-D-amino-acid retro-inverso peptide (not a classic growth factor or metabolic peptide) designed to disrupt the FOXO4-p53 protein-protein interaction in senescent cells, selectively triggering apoptosis in those cells while leaving healthy cells intact. It is an experimental senolytic compound with NO FDA approval and NO completed human clinical trials. All efficacy data derives from animal models and in vitro cell studies. Community use exists but lacks any clinical validation.
mechanism: FOXO4 (Forkhead box protein O4) is upregulated in senescent cells and physically binds to p53, sequestering it in the nucleus and preventing the p53-driven apoptosis that would normally clear those dysfunctional cells. FOXO4-DRI is an all-D-enantiomer retro-inverso peptide that competitively binds to p53's disordered transactivation domain 2 (TAD2), outcompeting endogenous FOXO4 for that binding site. Displacement of FOXO4 allows p53 to translocate out of the nucleus to the mitochondria, where it activates the intrinsic apoptosis cascade (involving BCL-2 family proteins and caspase-3 activation). Because FOXO4 expression is enriched in senescent cells relative to healthy proliferating cells, the apoptotic signal is preferentially directed at senescent cells. A 2025 NMR structural study (Nature Communications) confirmed the disordered p53-TAD2 as the primary binding site and showed that phosphorylation at Thr55 of p53-TAD2 dramatically enhances binding affinity.
monitoring_notes: Baseline inflammatory panel before any experimental use: IL-6, hsCRP, TNF-alpha, GDF-15 (emerging senescence surrogate marker) — reductions post-cycle would suggest SASP reduction and could serve as indirect efficacy signal
Complete blood count: senescent cells exist in bone marrow progenitor niches; monitor for unexpected cytopenias
Comprehensive metabolic panel including creatinine and BUN: renal function endpoint used in original mouse study (Baar 2017)
Testosterone and sex hormone panel in men: animal data (Zhang 2020) showed Leydig cell senescent population changes with FOXO4-DRI
Injection site assessment at each administration: document any granuloma, persistent erythema, or induration

No clinically validated biomarker panel for confirming senescent cell clearance exists in routine human practice as of 2025; specialty labs offer p16/p21 assays but clinical interpretation is not established
This compound has NO approved clinical monitoring protocol — all monitoring recommendations are extrapolated from research models and expert opinion
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy or personal history of cancer (p53 pathway engagement and removal of tumor-suppressive senescent cell populations creates theoretical cancer risk; no human safety data available)
Pregnancy or breastfeeding (no reproductive toxicity data in any species at clinically relevant doses)
Active acute wound healing or recent surgical procedure (senescent cell depletion may impair tissue repair mechanisms)
Known hypersensitivity to any component of the peptide sequence
Concurrent use of chemotherapy agents inducing senescence (timing interaction unstudied; chemotherapy-induced senescent cells may serve tumor-suppressive roles)
relative_contraindications: Immunocompromised states: unknown impact on immune-modulating senescent populations
Autoimmune conditions: SASP cytokine release from dying senescent cells could provoke inflammatory flare
Concurrent anticoagulation: injection site bleeding risk
Any condition requiring intact p53-mediated DNA damage responses (e.g., genetic predisposition syndromes such as Li-Fraumeni)
Advanced age with frailty: paradoxically, the individuals most likely to self-experiment are those at highest risk for adverse consequences of senescent cell depletion
common_side_effects: Injection site reactions: localized redness, burning, swelling, itching (self-resolving within 24-48 hours, anecdotal reports)
Transient fatigue and malaise following injection (anecdotal, possibly consistent with a senolytic inflammatory response as SASP contents are released from dying cells)
Muscle soreness (anecdotal community reports)
Transient nausea (anecdotal community reports)
Possible headache (anecdotal community reports)
serious_adverse_events: Off-target apoptosis in non-senescent cell populations: theoretically possible if FOXO4-p53 selectivity is incomplete; not well-characterized in any mammalian species at human-relevant doses

Impaired wound healing: senescent cells play a transiently beneficial role in tissue repair; systemic clearance of senescent cells could delay healing of acute wounds (ANIMAL/MECHANISTIC concern; see PMC6033032 review)

Immunosuppressive microenvironment disruption: senescent immune-modulating cells contribute to tumor surveillance; their removal may theoretically reduce cancer immune surveillance (MECHANISTIC concern)

p53 pathway perturbation risk: FOXO4-DRI physically engages p53, the most critical tumor suppressor in humans; any off-target modulation of p53 function in healthy cells carries theoretical oncogenic risk that cannot be excluded without human trial data
Unknown long-term consequences of repeated senolytic cycles in humans: no multi-year human data of any kind exists
Potential testosterone and endocrine disruption: animal data showed FOXO4-DRI altered Leydig cell populations and testosterone synthesis; human endocrine implications are unknown

dose_references.notes: This range reflects community/research-practice convention, not clinically validated. It is documented across secondary aggregator sources (e.g., PeptideDeck, PeptideWiki, ThePeptideCatalog, PeptideProtocolWiki) and has no human pharmacokinetic basis; it is an informal extrapolation from mouse data (5 mg/kg IP). No dose-ranging, safety, or efficacy data exist for any human dose. This row is provided solely as an educational reference range for the app's compound catalog and is NOT a recommendation. Clinician consultation is mandatory before any human use.

synergies.rationale: Dasatinib (a tyrosine kinase inhibitor) plus quercetin (a flavonoid) is the most-studied senolytic combination in human pilot trials (Kirkland et al., Mayo Clinic). D+Q targets BCL-2/BCL-XL anti-apoptotic proteins and tyrosine kinase survival pathways in senescent cells, which is mechanistically distinct from FOXO4-DRI's upstream FOXO4-p53 axis disruption. The two mechanisms are theoretically additive across different senescent cell survival pathways. No published co-administration data in any species exists; an in vitro protocol combining FOXO4-DRI with dasatinib has been described in non-peer-reviewed research-practice summaries only. This is an educational mechanistic note, not a usage recommendation.
synergies.caution_notes: Combining agents that drive apoptosis through overlapping but distinct pathways may increase off-target toxicity in tissues with high cellular turnover (GI epithelium, bone marrow). Dasatinib alone carries significant drug-interaction risk via CYP3A4 and has FDA labeling warnings for pleural effusion and QT prolongation. Any co-administration would require close clinical supervision.

synergies.rationale: Fisetin (a natural flavonoid) has shown senolytic activity in mouse models and one small human pilot study for frailty. Like quercetin, it inhibits BCL-2-family anti-apoptotic proteins, which are downstream effectors in the p53 apoptosis pathway that FOXO4-DRI engages. Mechanistically the two may act on overlapping but non-identical senescent cell subpopulations. Purely mechanistic rationale; no co-administration data exists. Educational note only, not a usage recommendation.
synergies.caution_notes: Fisetin's human efficacy data is very limited (one small pilot RCT). Stacking two senolytics amplifies the theoretical risk of excessive senescent cell clearance and impaired wound healing. No safety data exist for combination use.

synergies.rationale: NAD+ precursors (nicotinamide mononucleotide or nicotinamide riboside) support mitochondrial biogenesis and cellular energy metabolism via SIRT1/SIRT3 pathway activation and have reduced SASP markers in some cell models. The mechanistic hypothesis for pairing with FOXO4-DRI is that clearing senescent cells while supporting mitochondrial function in remaining healthy cells could reinforce a tissue-rejuvenation signal. Speculative and purely mechanistic; no combination data published. Educational note only.
synergies.caution_notes: NAD+ precursors are generally considered low-risk supplements with a reasonable human safety profile in short-term RCTs. The synergy hypothesis is speculative; no co-administration studies exist with FOXO4-DRI.

name: GDF-8 / Myostatin (Myostatin Propeptide)
short_description: GDF-8 (myostatin) is an endogenous TGF-beta superfamily protein that functions as the primary negative regulator of skeletal muscle mass. In the research and performance-enhancement context, what is typically called "GDF-8" or "Myostatin Propeptide" refers to the exogenous recombinant N-terminal propeptide of myostatin, which re-binds to mature myostatin and prevents it from activating the ActRIIB receptor, thereby disinhibiting muscle growth. This compound is NOT FDA-approved for any indication, is banned by WADA, has NO established human therapeutic dosing, and all muscle-mass data for the propeptide itself come from animal models. Multiple pharmaceutical-grade myostatin/ActRIIB inhibitors (monoclonal antibodies, receptor decoys) have entered human clinical trials for muscular dystrophy and sarcopenia and largely failed to meet primary efficacy endpoints. This entry is provided strictly for educational, evidence-graded cataloging. It is not medical advice and does not constitute a dosing recommendation; any compound use should be discussed with a qualified clinician.
mechanism: Myostatin (GDF-8) is synthesized as an inactive precursor; the N-terminal propeptide domain cleaves from the C-terminal signaling domain but remains non-covalently associated, holding mature myostatin in a latent complex. Proteolytic cleavage by BMP-1/tolloid-family metalloproteases at Asp-76 releases bioactive mature myostatin, which then binds with high affinity to activin type IIB receptor (ActRIIB) on skeletal myofibers. ActRIIB engagement recruits type I receptors ALK4/ALK5, which phosphorylate SMAD2 and SMAD3. Phospho-SMAD2/3 complexes with SMAD4 and translocates to the nucleus, where it upregulates atrophy-related ubiquitin ligases (Atrogin-1, MuRF1) and suppresses satellite cell proliferation, collectively reducing muscle fiber size and inhibiting hypertrophy. Exogenous recombinant myostatin propeptide re-binds to the mature domain, reconstituting the latent complex and preventing ActRIIB engagement, functionally inhibiting myostatin signaling. Additionally, circulating endogenous inhibitors follistatin (FST), follistatin-like 3 (FSTL-3), and GASP-1 regulate myostatin availability extracellularly. Myostatin is also expressed in cardiac muscle and may modulate cardiac energy homeostasis, making systemic inhibition potentially relevant beyond skeletal muscle. Secondary metabolic effects of myostatin loss observed in genetic-knockout and pharmacological-inhibition models include reduced adipogenesis, improved insulin sensitivity, and lower circulating triglycerides.
monitoring_notes: Cardiac evaluation (ECG, echocardiogram) at baseline and periodically, given cardiac myostatin expression and uncertain long-term cardiac effects
Bone mineral density (DEXA) at baseline and at intervals, especially with follistatin-based strategies, given risk of reduced BMD through GDF11 antagonism
Comprehensive metabolic panel and CBC at baseline to detect pre-existing conditions that may be exacerbated
Serum FSH and LH in reproductive-age individuals, since ActRIIB-pathway modulation can suppress gonadotropins
Ophthalmologic assessment if ocular symptoms develop, given documented retinal fluid leakage (central serous chorioretinopathy) with follistatin SC use
Monitor for bleeding symptoms (epistaxis, gum bleeding, easy bruising), given vascular TGF-beta cross-inhibition risk
Functional strength assessment (e.g., grip strength, timed walk), since human trials show muscle-mass increases without proportional strength gains

No standardized clinical monitoring protocol exists for research-grade myostatin propeptide; the above is extrapolated from pharmaceutical-grade clinical-trial safety monitoring and is general education, not a care plan. Any monitoring should be directed by a qualified clinician.
absolute_contraindications: Active or suspected malignancy: myostatin has anti-proliferative effects on some tumor cells and inhibition could theoretically remove a tumor-suppressive signal; insufficient data to rule out risk

Pregnancy and breastfeeding: myostatin is expressed in placental tissue, uterus, and oocytes and plays roles in steroidogenesis, embryo implantation, and placental glucose metabolism; inhibition could disrupt normal reproductive physiology (PMC9250276)
Known hypersensitivity to the recombinant protein or excipients

Pediatric and adolescent use: no safety or dose data; developmental roles of myostatin in muscle-fiber-number determination during embryogenesis and early postnatal growth make inhibition in growing individuals particularly uncertain
relative_contraindications: Competitive athletes subject to WADA testing: myostatin inhibitors are on the WADA Prohibited List (S4: Hormone and Metabolic Modulators); use results in an anti-doping rule violation
Pre-existing cardiac disease or cardiomyopathy: cardiac myostatin expression and uncertain long-term cardiac effects warrant caution; baseline and follow-up cardiac evaluation is prudent
Osteoporosis or low bone density: follistatin-based and broad-spectrum inhibitors may reduce bone mineral density through GDF11 antagonism
Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) or known reproductive-hormone dysregulation: myostatin modulates granulosa-cell function and ovarian steroidogenesis
Concurrent use of immunosuppressants: theoretical additive risk from biologics-class compounds affecting TGF-beta family signaling
History of bleeding disorders or anticoagulant use: vascular adverse events were observed in clinical trials of related compounds

Pre-existing tendinopathy or connective-tissue disorder: disproportionate muscle-mass gain without commensurate tendon adaptation is a theoretical mechanical-mismatch risk of myostatin/ActRIIB inhibition; unstudied in humans.
common_side_effects: Nosebleeds (epistaxis), reported in clinical trials of broader ActRIIB antagonists
Gum bleeding, reported with ramatercept/ACE-031 in Phase 2, attributed to BMP-9/10 cross-inhibition
Telangiectasia and skin erythema, vascular effects from off-target TGF-beta inhibition
Muscle spasms or cramping, reported anecdotally
Retinal fluid leakage (central serous chorioretinopathy), documented in a case series of bodybuilders who self-injected follistatin-344
Potential tendon weakness relative to disproportionate muscle hypertrophy, theoretical but raised in preclinical safety analyses
FSH suppression, with broader receptor-trap antagonists affecting activin signaling
serious_adverse_events: Vascular endothelial damage (ACE-031/ramatercept was discontinued in Phase 2 due to nosebleeds, gum bleeding, telangiectasia, and erythema, attributed to cross-inhibition of BMP-9/10 which regulate endothelial function)

Cardiac hypertrophy or fibrosis: myostatin is expressed in cardiac muscle and may have cardioprotective roles; long-term systemic-inhibition effects on the human heart are NOT well-characterized, and myostatin-null mice develop myocardial fibrosis at advanced ages in some studies

Spontaneous bone fractures: follistatin overexpression in animals blocked GDF11 (a myostatin paralog that promotes osteogenesis), decreased bone mineral density, and caused spontaneous fractures; relevant to follistatin-based myostatin-inhibition strategies
Hormonal-axis disruption: ActRIIB-targeting agents caused significant FSH reductions in clinical trials, indicating systemic activin-signaling disruption

Unknown long-term oncological risk: myostatin has complex dual roles in cancer biology (it can promote cachexia but also has anti-proliferative/pro-apoptotic effects on some cancer cells); systemic inhibition in the context of undiagnosed malignancy carries undefined risk

Product purity and contamination risk: black-market recombinant proteins carry risk of endotoxin, misfolding, and microbial contamination; WADA has documented significant content variability in seized products

citation.title: Administration study of black market myostatin propeptide

dose_references.notes: This 100-300 mcg SC range reflects community/research-practice convention, not clinically validated. It is NOT derived from any human clinical trial, has NO established human safety profile at these doses, and carries no endorsement. The animal literature uses mg/kg systemic doses orders of magnitude higher on a per-weight basis, so these figures cannot be reconciled with the preclinical data. A WADA-funded analysis found significant content/purity variability in black-market myostatin propeptide products. This row exists solely to document what circulates in research communities and must not be interpreted as a dosing recommendation.

synergies.rationale: Follistatin is the primary endogenous extracellular inhibitor of myostatin, binding and neutralizing it before ActRIIB engagement, and it also suppresses activin A, which shares compensatory signaling with myostatin in human muscle. The educational interest in combining a myostatin propeptide (targeting mature myostatin) with follistatin (a broader TGF-beta antagonist) is the concept of dual-node pathway suppression. In animal models, follistatin overexpression produces larger muscle-mass increases than myostatin-specific inhibition alone, and human resistance-training studies show naturally coordinated follistatin upregulation alongside myostatin suppression (PMC6425763). This is presented as mechanistic context, not a protocol.
synergies.caution_notes: Follistatin has broad off-target effects across the TGF-beta family, including inhibition of GDF11 (osteogenesis), BMP-9/10 (endothelial function), and activins (FSH regulation). Animal data show follistatin overexpression can decrease bone mineral density and cause spontaneous fractures. A case series flagged by Poison Control described bodybuilders who self-injected follistatin-344 and developed central serous chorioretinopathy (retinal fluid leakage). Combining these compounds would amplify off-target TGF-beta suppression and is not validated in humans; discuss any such interest with a clinician.

synergies.rationale: Mechanistically complementary at the pathway level: myostatin inhibition removes the ActRIIB-SMAD2/3 brake on muscle growth, while IGF-1 activates the PI3K-Akt-mTORC1 anabolic cascade. Follistatin-induced hypertrophy in animal models depends on insulin/IGF-1 pathway activation downstream of PI3K/Akt, suggesting the pathways are non-redundant. In vitro, combined IGF-1 LR3 plus follistatin-344 produced additive myotube-diameter increases. Presented as mechanistic rationale only.
synergies.caution_notes: No controlled human data on combined use. IGF-1 LR3 carries its own risk profile including hypoglycemia and theoretical mitogenic concerns. Stacking it with a myostatin inhibitor adds physiological complexity and unpredictability. Educational context only; not a recommendation.

synergies.rationale: Progressive resistance training endogenously downregulates circulating myostatin and upregulates follistatin, the same pathway axis targeted by exogenous inhibitors. A human RCT in elderly sarcopenic men (n=31, 8 weeks) showed resistance training decreased myostatin and increased follistatin in both healthy and sarcopenic groups (PMC6425763). Pharmaceutical myostatin/ActRIIB inhibitor trials (e.g., bimagrumab, landogrozumab) consistently show that mass gains without exercise fail to translate into functional improvement, underscoring that exercise is the best-validated, lowest-risk way to engage this pathway.
synergies.caution_notes: Exercise is the only validated human strategy for modulating the myostatin pathway with an established safety profile. Exogenous inhibitor use remains unvalidated and carries safety risks not present with exercise alone.

synergies.rationale: BPC-157 is hypothesized to support tendon and connective-tissue integrity via upregulation of growth-hormone-receptor expression and local angiogenesis. This is mechanistically relevant to a theoretical safety concern of myostatin inhibition: disproportionate muscle growth relative to tendon and bone strength, creating a potential mechanical mismatch. The idea that tendon-repair signaling could offset that mismatch is offered purely as a hypothesis.
synergies.caution_notes: Entirely mechanistic/hypothetical. No human or animal data on co-administration. BPC-157 itself lacks human RCT data. The tendon-protection rationale is plausible but unvalidated; not a recommendation.

name: Pinealon
short_description: Pinealon (EDR) is a synthetic tripeptide (Glu-Asp-Arg, MW ~418 Da) developed by Vladimir Khavinson's group at the St. Petersburg Institute of Bioregulation and Gerontology. It is classified as a peptide bioregulator and studied primarily for neuroprotection, anti-aging, and cognitive preservation. NOT FDA-approved. Not an approved drug in any major Western jurisdiction. Research exists almost entirely from a single Russian academic lineage; independent Western replication is essentially absent. Framed here for educational reference only — not a prescription or clinical recommendation.
mechanism: Pinealon operates through several proposed, partially overlapping mechanisms supported by in vitro and animal studies: (1) Antioxidant pathway activation — dose-dependent suppression of reactive oxygen species (ROS) accumulation in neuronal cells, with increased expression of antioxidant enzymes SOD2 and GPx1. (2) MAPK/ERK pathway modulation — delayed ERK1/2 activation under oxidative stress conditions, reducing necrotic cell death. (3) Direct nuclear/DNA interaction — owing to its small size (~418 Da), EDR is proposed to cross lipid bilayers including the nuclear membrane and interact with promoter regions of PPARA, PPARG, and TPH1 genes, potentially activating serotonin synthesis and neuronal gene expression programs. (4) Apoptosis reduction — decreased caspase-3 and p53 expression in animal models of hypoxia. (5) Dendritic spine preservation — in 5xFAD transgenic mouse models, EDR increased mushroom-spine density by ~11%, restoring spine density toward control levels. Note: a receptor for Pinealon has not been identified; the direct DNA-interaction model remains mechanistic/speculative and requires independent validation.

name: PNC-27
short_description: PNC-27 is a synthetic 32-amino-acid chimeric research peptide — not a body-recomposition or longevity peptide — engineered by fusing residues 12–26 of the human p53 tumor suppressor protein with a penetratin cell-penetrating domain. It was developed at SUNY Downstate Medical Center around 2000. Its only studied application is selective cancer cell killing in preclinical (in vitro and animal) models. It has never entered human clinical trials, is not FDA-approved for any indication, and has been the subject of FDA safety warnings, bacterial contamination findings, and a criminal prosecution. No body-recomposition or metabolic benefit has been identified in any peer-reviewed literature.
mechanism: PNC-27 targets human double minute 2 (HDM-2 / MDM2) protein, which is aberrantly expressed on the plasma membranes of many cancer cell types but is absent from the membranes of normal untransformed cells. The p53-derived N-terminal domain (residues 12–26) binds the hydrophobic cleft of membrane-bound HDM-2 in a conformation that mirrors native p53–MDM2 interaction (key contact residues: Phe19, Trp23, Leu26). Once bound, PNC-27:HDM-2 complexes oligomerize into ring-shaped transmembrane pore structures (~28–44 nm diameter). Pore formation causes rapid necrotic lysis (not apoptosis) with extrusion of intracellular contents — a process termed "poptosis." A 2024 study additionally identified a secondary mechanism: PNC-27 enters cancer cells and selectively disrupts mitochondrial membranes, amplifying cell death. Because HDM-2 membrane localization is restricted to transformed cells, normal cells are largely spared in preclinical models. This selectivity is reported to be p53-status independent. All mechanistic data derive from in vitro and animal models; none has been confirmed in humans.
monitoring_notes: No validated monitoring protocol exists for PNC-27 in humans because no human clinical trials have been conducted.

If a clinician is supervising experimental use in a research context, complete blood count, comprehensive metabolic panel, and hepatic function panels would be standard precautions given the total absence of human toxicology data.
Any signs of infection — fever, rigors, erythema at an injection site — should prompt immediate medical evaluation given documented contamination risk in unregulated supply chains.
Gastrointestinal symptoms including melena, hematemesis, or unexplained blood loss require immediate evaluation given the published case report of GI hemorrhage.
Users should be counseled that the FDA has issued formal warnings against use of PNC-27 products and that all commercially available PNC-27 is marketed without FDA review or approval.
absolute_contraindications: Any use outside a formally supervised, IRB-approved, FDA-authorized clinical trial — PNC-27 has no approved human indication and no established human safety profile.
Use as a cancer treatment outside regulated clinical research — the FDA has explicitly warned consumers not to use marketed PNC-27 products.
Use in immunocompromised individuals, given documented bacterial contamination risk in unregulated preparations.
Pregnancy or breastfeeding — no safety data exist for any population, especially vulnerable groups.
relative_contraindications: Active gastrointestinal conditions — given the single published case report of GI hemorrhage temporally associated with PNC-27 use.
Known or suspected cancer — experimental use without oncology supervision is dangerous and legally problematic in the US.
Any concurrent chemotherapy or immunosuppressive therapy — no interaction data exist.
common_side_effects: Unknown — no human clinical trial data exist, so no established human adverse-event profile has been published. Items below are theoretical or derived from a single case report and contamination incidents.
Potential injection-site reactions (theoretical, extrapolated from the peptide class)
Gastrointestinal disturbance — one case report documented massive GI hemorrhage temporally associated with PNC-27 use (causation unproven; patient had end-stage metastatic cancer)
Bacterial infection risk from contaminated unregulated preparations (Variovorax paradoxus contamination detected by FDA, 2017)
serious_adverse_events: Massive GI hemorrhage: A 2017 case report in the American Journal of Gastroenterology described a 46-year-old woman with end-stage metastatic cervical cancer who received PNC-27 experimentally in Mexico and subsequently developed massive GI bleeding requiring repeated transfusions; she died after transition to comfort care. Causation was not established but the temporal association raised concern.

Serious infection risk from contaminated unregulated preparations: FDA laboratory testing found Variovorax paradoxus bacteria in a marketed PNC-27 inhalation solution sample (FDA warning, January 2017). Contaminated products can cause life-threatening infections, particularly in immunocompromised individuals.
Unknown oncological and systemic risks: No data exist on whether PNC-27 might affect normal tissues expressing low-level MDM-2, or on long-term systemic toxicity in humans.

Criminal prosecution for fraudulent manufacture: A Birmingham, AL operator was sentenced to 30 months in federal prison (2021) for manufacturing adulterated PNC-27 suppositories in non-sterile kitchen conditions and selling them as cancer cures; products were found to contain foreign materials including hair.

name: IGF-1 DES (des(1-3)IGF-1)
short_description: des(1-3)IGF-1 is a naturally occurring, endogenous truncated analog of insulin-like growth factor-1, lacking the first three N-terminal amino acids (Gly-Pro-Glu), first isolated from human brain tissue. This structural change reduces binding to IGF binding proteins (IGFBPs), producing greater in-vivo potency than native IGF-1 in animal models and a very short half-life (~20–30 min). It is a research-only peptide: NOT FDA-approved for any indication, NOT approved for human therapeutic use, and explicitly prohibited by WADA (S2 category) in all sports. All human use outside formally approved clinical trials is experimental. The only FDA-approved IGF-1 product is mecasermin (Increlex), a full-length recombinant human IGF-1, indicated only for severe primary IGF-1 deficiency (growth failure) in pediatric patients; that approval does NOT extend to truncated analogs.
mechanism: des(1-3)IGF-1 is generated endogenously by N-terminal proteolytic cleavage of native IGF-1, removing the tripeptide Gly-Pro-Glu. This truncation dramatically reduces binding affinity for IGF binding proteins (IGFBPs), particularly IGFBP-3, so a much larger fraction of the peptide circulates in free, bioavailable form rather than being sequestered in the IGFBP reservoir. It binds and activates the IGF-1 receptor (IGF-1R), triggering downstream PI3K/Akt/mTOR and MAPK/ERK signaling cascades, which drive protein synthesis, cell survival, myoblast proliferation, and satellite cell activation. IGF-1R/insulin receptor hybrid signaling also contributes to glucose uptake. Because of the very short half-life (~20–30 min) and reduced IGFBP binding, intramuscular injection is theorized to produce a localized, site-specific anabolic stimulus rather than the sustained systemic exposure seen with longer-acting IGF-1 analogs such as IGF-1 LR3. All supporting evidence for this localized hyperplasia model derives from animal or in-vitro data; no controlled human studies of this form specifically have been conducted.
monitoring_notes: Fasting and post-injection blood glucose monitoring — especially during any initiation period; avoid unsupervised strenuous activity within 2–3 hours of administration
Serum IGF-1 levels — to assess exposure and detect supratherapeutic accumulation
HbA1c and fasting insulin — to monitor for insulin resistance development
Funduscopic examination — screen for intracranial hypertension (papilledema) as documented with mecasermin class
Cardiovascular assessment — baseline and periodic echocardiogram for any extended use given cardiomegaly risk from IGF-1 excess
Oncologic surveillance — any unexplained mass, lymphadenopathy, or cancer-risk symptoms warrant immediate evaluation and discontinuation
Injection site inspection — for signs of infection, particularly given non-pharmaceutical-grade preparation concerns
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy or personal history of malignancy (IGF-1R is pro-oncogenic; mecasermin label lists this as an absolute contraindication)
Closed epiphyses in growing children (bone plate fusion risk)
Known hypersensitivity to IGF-1 or any component of the preparation
Pregnancy or breastfeeding (no safety data; IGF-1 axis dysregulation in fetal development is a serious risk)
relative_contraindications: Diabetes mellitus — heightened hypoglycemia risk requiring close glycemic monitoring
Pre-diabetic states or insulin resistance
Strong family history of hormone-sensitive cancers (prostate, breast, colorectal)
Concurrent use of insulin or insulin secretagogues (additive hypoglycemia)
Elevated baseline IGF-1 levels
History of intracranial hypertension or papilledema
Sleep apnea (lymphoid tissue hypertrophy documented with mecasermin class)
Competitive athletes in WADA-governed sports (prohibited substance, S2 category, in- and out-of-competition)
common_side_effects: Hypoglycemia (most common acute risk — IGF-1 cross-activates insulin receptors and hybrid IGF-1R/insulin receptor heterodimers, driving glucose uptake)
Injection site pain, erythema, or induration
Headache
Dizziness or light-headedness (from glycemic fluctuations)
Fatigue
Water retention / edema (extrapolated from IGF-1 class effects)
Jaw or extremity growth with chronic supraphysiological exposure (acromegaloid risk — class effect)
serious_adverse_events: Severe hypoglycemia with loss of consciousness or seizure — documented with FDA-approved mecasermin (Increlex) at therapeutic doses and extrapolated to IGF-1 DES; risk is amplified by the reduced-IGFBP-binding, more-bioavailable form

Promotion of pre-existing malignancy or occult cancer — IGF-1R signaling drives proliferation, anti-apoptosis, angiogenesis, and metastasis in multiple cancer types (breast, prostate, colorectal, hepatocellular); chronic agonism is a plausible but unquantified risk with des(1-3)IGF-1
Intracranial hypertension (papilledema, vision changes) — documented with mecasermin class

Cardiomegaly / acromegalic cardiomyopathy with chronic supraphysiological IGF-1R activation (biventricular hypertrophy, diastolic dysfunction) — well-established in acromegaly literature; theoretical risk with prolonged exogenous use
Organomegaly (visceral organ enlargement) — known consequence of sustained IGF-1 excess extrapolated from acromegaly data
Anaphylaxis / systemic allergic reaction — documented with mecasermin class; unknown rate with research-grade preparations
Unknown risks from uncharacterized purity and sterility of research-grade (non-pharmaceutical) preparations

synergies.rationale: GHRH analogs stimulate pituitary GH release, which drives hepatic IGF-1 production. synergies.caution_notes: MGF peptide's independent bioactivity is not robustly established in human trials. Combining two research-only, unapproved peptides amplifies both safety uncertainty and compounding regulatory/quality concerns. Not for human use outside formally approved studies.

synergies.rationale: BPC-157 is a synthetic pentadecapeptide studied for tissue repair and angiogenesis in animal models. synergies.caution_notes: Both compounds lack human clinical trial data for body-recomposition use. BPC-157 is itself unapproved for human use. Combination safety is entirely unknown.

name: N-Acetyl Epitalon Amidate
short_description: N-Acetyl Epitalon Amidate is a chemically stabilized, end-capped synthetic derivative of Epitalon (AEDG tetrapeptide: Ala-Glu-Asp-Gly), itself derived from Epithalamin — a crude bovine pineal-gland polypeptide extract developed by Russian gerontologist Vladimir Khavinson. N-terminal acetylation and C-terminal amidation are standard peptide-chemistry techniques intended to slow exopeptidase degradation and extend half-life versus the unmodified base peptide; no published pharmacokinetic study has validated this advantage for the amidate form in humans. The base peptide Epitalon has the largest evidence base (predominantly from a single Russian research group); N-Acetyl Epitalon Amidate as a distinct chemical entity has no independent clinical or animal study record. NOT FDA-approved; sold in the United States as a research chemical only. Scheduled for FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) review in July 2026 for potential 503A compounding-list consideration.
mechanism: The base tetrapeptide AEDG (Ala-Glu-Asp-Gly) is hypothesized to act through multiple overlapping pathways. (1) Telomerase activation: in vitro studies using human fetal fibroblasts showed Epitalon increased expression of the catalytic telomerase subunit (hTERT) and extended telomere length beyond the Hayflick limit; a 2025 Biogerontology study (Al-dulaimi et al.) confirmed telomere lengthening in normal human cell lines via hTERT upregulation and, separately, ALT (Alternative Lengthening of Telomeres) activation in cancer cell lines. (2) Epigenetic / chromatin interaction: molecular modeling and in vitro work (Khavinson et al., 2020, Molecules) indicates AEDG binds linker histones H1/3 and H1/6 at DNA-interacting sites, potentially competing with histones to increase transcription probability of neurogenesis and anti-senescence genes. (3) Pineal neuroendocrine modulation: Epithalamin (the parent polypeptide extract) stimulates melatonin synthesis in elderly adults with pineal dysfunction; evidence for synthetic Epitalon on melatonin is conflicting across species. (4) Antioxidant upregulation: preclinical data show increased superoxide dismutase, glutathione peroxidase, and catalase activity. The N-acetyl amidate form is expected to share these mechanisms; the chemical modifications are pharmacokinetic in intent (protease resistance, charge neutralization) rather than mechanistic — no head-to-head mechanistic study of amidate vs. base form has been published.
monitoring_notes: No clinically established monitoring protocol exists for Epitalon or N-Acetyl Epitalon Amidate
Clinician discussion and baseline health assessment are strongly advised before use given research-only status
Individuals with cancer risk factors should consult an oncologist given theoretical telomerase concerns

Users should source only from suppliers that provide third-party certificates of analysis (CoA) verifying purity and sterility; no regulatory body oversees research-peptide manufacturing quality in the US
Telomere length testing (e.g., commercial leukocyte telomere length assays) is available but clinical utility of monitoring telomere length in response to this compound is not established
Monitor for injection-site reactions; rotate injection sites
Schedule for FDA PCAC review: July 23-24, 2026 — clinicians should monitor for regulatory updates affecting compounding legality
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy or personal history of hormone-sensitive or rapidly proliferating cancer (theoretical telomerase-activation risk; no clinical data to quantify this risk)
Known hypersensitivity to any component of the peptide or its excipients
relative_contraindications: Personal or strong family history of cancer (theoretical telomerase concern warrants clinician discussion before use)
Pregnancy or breastfeeding (no safety data exist)
Pediatric use (no data; aging-related indication is not applicable)
Concurrent use of immunomodulatory agents or cancer chemotherapy (no interaction data)
Autoimmune conditions (immune modulation hypothesized; no data)
Bovine-derived product allergy: Epithalamin (parent extract) is bovine-derived; synthetic amidate form does not carry this risk if confirmed synthetically pure, but sourcing verification is required
common_side_effects: Injection site redness, swelling, or transient irritation (reported in community use of base Epitalon; attributed to SC/IM administration technique)
Fatigue or somnolence, particularly if administered in the evening
Vivid or unusual dreams (plausibly related to hypothesized melatonin pathway modulation)
Mild headache
Mild nausea
serious_adverse_events: No severe adverse events reported in the two 3-year Russian epithalamin clinical trials (ADDF Cognitive Vitality Report, 2015); however, these trials used the crude bovine pineal extract, not synthetic Epitalon or the amidate form, and they lacked modern pharmacovigilance standards

Theoretical oncologic risk from telomerase activation in individuals with occult or active malignancy: telomerase is upregulated in the majority of human cancers; a compound that further activates it could theoretically support tumor cell proliferation, though published Epitalon preclinical studies paradoxically showed reduced tumor incidence in cancer-prone mice (not validated independently)

Risk from unregulated synthesis: purity and sterility of research-grade N-Acetyl Epitalon Amidate from non-pharmaceutical suppliers are not independently verified; impurities could pose unpredictable safety risks
No Phase 1 pharmacokinetic or maximum-tolerated-dose study has been conducted for Epitalon or its amidate derivative in humans

name: SNAP-8 (Acetyl Octapeptide-3)
short_description: SNAP-8 (Acetyl Octapeptide-3) is a synthetic cosmeceutical octapeptide designed for topical application to reduce the appearance of dynamic facial wrinkles. It is NOT a classic body-recomposition or longevity peptide and has no established systemic use. It is not FDA-approved as a drug and is not indicated for injection, oral, or any non-topical route. All available clinical evidence is from manufacturer-sponsored cosmetic studies or small observational cohorts, not independent randomized controlled trials. Its mechanism involves competing with the endogenous protein SNAP-25 for a binding site in the neuronal SNARE complex, thereby attenuating acetylcholine release and facial muscle contraction. It is cataloged here in the educational context of topical skin-health compounds relevant to longevity-adjacent aesthetics, with clinician disclaimer required on all display surfaces.
mechanism: SNAP-8 is an eight-amino-acid synthetic peptide (sequence: Ac-Glu-Glu-Met-Gln-Arg-Arg-Ala-Asp-NH2) that structurally mimics the N-terminal fragment of SNAP-25, a component of the neuronal SNARE (Soluble NSF Attachment Protein REceptor) complex. The SNARE complex — comprising SNAP-25, syntaxin-1, and VAMP (vesicle-associated membrane protein) — is required for docking and fusion of synaptic vesicles with the presynaptic membrane, which is the final step before calcium-triggered neurotransmitter (primarily acetylcholine) exocytosis. By competing with native SNAP-25 for the VAMP binding site, SNAP-8 destabilizes ternary SNARE complex assembly, reducing the efficiency of vesicular exocytosis at the neuromuscular junction. This attenuates the frequency and force of facial muscle contractions driven by repetitive expression, thereby reducing dynamic wrinkle formation. In vitro data (Institute of Advanced Chemistry of Catalonia, manufacturer-reported) indicates glutamate release is reduced approximately 43% at 1.5 mM concentration. Importantly, the degree of topical penetration through the stratum corneum to reach the neuromuscular junction is poorly established; the anti-wrinkle effects observed clinically may partly reflect epidermal hydration and altered skin surface texture rather than confirmed neuromuscular relaxation. SNAP-8 is an extended (two-residue longer) analog of Argireline (Acetyl Hexapeptide-3/8), theorized to confer greater binding affinity to the SNARE complex and improved peptide stability.
monitoring_notes: No specific laboratory or clinical monitoring required for topical cosmetic use at standard concentrations
Patch test on inner forearm 24–48 hours prior to facial application is recommended, particularly in individuals with sensitive or reactive skin
Observe for signs of allergic contact dermatitis (persistent redness, pruritus, vesiculation) within the first 2 weeks of use; discontinue if these occur
Monitor skin barrier integrity if combining with retinol, AHAs, or other sensitizing actives — increased TEWL or persistent dryness warrants reduction in active load
No blood work, imaging, or systemic monitoring is indicated or relevant for topical cosmetic use
absolute_contraindications: Known hypersensitivity or allergic reaction to SNAP-8 or any component of the formulation

Injectable (sc/im/iv) use: SNAP-8 has no validated injectable dosing protocol, no sterile pharmaceutical preparation approval, and no safety data for systemic or injectable administration; injectable use is absolutely contraindicated
Application to open wounds, broken skin, or active infectious dermatosis
relative_contraindications: Pregnancy and breastfeeding: no safety data exist for topical SNAP-8 in pregnancy or lactation; precautionary avoidance is advised pending evidence
Active inflammatory skin conditions (rosacea, eczema, psoriasis flares): risk of exacerbated irritation; consult dermatologist before use
Concurrent use of aggressive chemical exfoliants (AHAs, BHAs, retinoids) without dermatologist guidance — may compromise skin barrier and increase irritant absorption
Periocular use in individuals with dry eye disease or who wear contact lenses: assess formulation compatibility with ophthalmologist if applying very close to the eye
common_side_effects: Mild transient skin irritation or erythema (particularly in sensitive skin types or with DIY formulations at incorrect pH)
Temporary tingling or stinging sensation on application (self-resolving within minutes)
Dry skin or mild flaking (typically formulation-vehicle related rather than peptide-specific)
Oily or tacky skin sensation depending on carrier/vehicle used
serious_adverse_events: No serious adverse events have been reported with topical SNAP-8 application in published literature

Mycobacterium abscessus cutaneous infection has been reported with intradermal injection of the related compound acetyl hexapeptide-8 (Argireline) — this risk applies to ANY injectable use of cosmetic-grade peptide solutions that are not sterile pharmaceutical preparations; SNAP-8 is not indicated for injection under any evidence-based protocol
Rare allergic contact sensitization is theoretically possible as with any novel peptide applied repetitively to skin; not formally quantified
