synergies.rationale: Leuphasyl mimics enkephalins and acts on opioid receptors at the presynaptic nerve terminal, reducing neuronal excitability and modulating acetylcholine release through a G-protein-coupled receptor pathway. SNAP-8 acts downstream at the SNARE complex level. In vitro data cited in PMC7662462 reports SNAP-8 alone achieves approximately 38% neurotransmitter inhibition and Leuphasyl alone approximately 7%, with additive/synergistic inhibition (approximately 47%) reported when combined. The complementary mechanisms (receptor-level vs. vesicle-fusion-level) provide an educational rationale for why the two are sometimes co-formulated. Educational framing only; no independent RCT has validated this combination in humans.
synergies.caution_notes: Combination data is from in vitro assays cited in a manufacturer-adjacent review; no independent human RCT. Combination may increase risk of mild skin irritation in sensitive individuals. Educational information only; not a recommended protocol.

synergies.rationale: Argireline is the predecessor hexapeptide targeting the same SNAP-25/SNARE mechanism. SNAP-8 was designed as an extended analog with potentially greater SNARE binding affinity. The two are sometimes co-formulated in the cosmetic industry under a multi-epitope competition rationale, though marginal benefit over higher-concentration SNAP-8 alone is not demonstrated in independent studies. Because both are SNAP-25 mimetics targeting the same site, the mechanistic rationale beyond increased competitive concentration is limited. Educational framing only.
synergies.caution_notes: No independent RCT evidence for superiority over single-agent SNAP-8 at equivalent total peptide concentration. Risk of formulation instability if pH or ionic conditions are not optimized for both peptides simultaneously. Educational information only.

synergies.rationale: Matrixyl compounds (matrikine peptides) act via TGF-beta receptor and fibronectin receptor signaling to stimulate fibroblast synthesis of collagen I, III, and IV, and hyaluronic acid — addressing structural volume loss and ECM degradation that contribute to static wrinkles. SNAP-8 addresses dynamic wrinkle formation through SNARE inhibition. These mechanisms are orthogonal: one targets expression-driven groove formation, the other targets dermal structural deficit, which is why they are commonly co-formulated in commercial anti-aging serums. A double-blind RCT (PMC10005804) studied palmitoyl pentapeptide-4 alongside acetyl hexapeptide-3 for crow's feet; the human evidence is for the individual agents, not for any SNAP-8-containing combination. Educational framing only.
synergies.caution_notes: The cited RCT evidence is for individual agents, NOT the SNAP-8 + Matrixyl combination, which remains untested in controlled trials. Treat combination claims as mechanistic only. Educational information only.

synergies.rationale: GHK-Cu (glycyl-L-histidyl-L-lysine copper complex) is an endogenous tripeptide-copper complex that stimulates collagen, elastin, and glycosaminoglycan synthesis via fibroblast activation and modulates metalloproteinase activity for ECM remodeling. Like Matrixyl, it addresses the structural component of skin aging rather than neuromuscular dynamics. The two are theorized to be complementary — structural skin repair (GHK-Cu) plus dynamic wrinkle attenuation (SNAP-8). There is a mechanistic basis for complementarity, but no controlled human study has directly tested this combination. Educational framing only.
synergies.caution_notes: GHK-Cu has its own evidence base for wound healing and skin remodeling (multiple human studies), but the combination with SNAP-8 specifically is not studied in controlled trials. Formulation compatibility (pH, ionic competition with the copper complex) requires attention. Educational information only.

synergies.rationale: Retinol increases epidermal cell turnover and stimulates dermal collagen synthesis via retinoic acid receptor (RAR) signaling; it is among the best-evidenced topical anti-aging actives. It is commonly co-formulated with peptides such as SNAP-8 to address both dynamic wrinkle attenuation (SNAP-8 via SNARE inhibition) and collagen remodeling / cell renewal (retinol). The mechanisms are orthogonal and theoretically additive. No independent controlled study has tested the specific combination. Educational framing only.
synergies.caution_notes: Retinol causes skin sensitization, especially at initiation. Combining with SNAP-8 may increase the likelihood of mild irritation, erythema, or dryness in sensitive skin. pH matters: retinol formulations (~5.5) and peptide formulations (~6–7) may have incompatible optimal pH ranges; application sequencing and formulation stability should be verified. Educational information only.

name: Vitamin B12 (Methylcobalamin)
short_description: Vitamin B12 is an essential water-soluble vitamin (not a peptide or growth factor) required for DNA synthesis, neurological function, and one-carbon methylation metabolism. Methylcobalamin (MeCbl) is the active coenzyme form that functions directly as a cofactor for methionine synthase without requiring hepatic conversion. Injectable cyanocobalamin and hydroxocobalamin are FDA-approved pharmaceutical forms for B12 deficiency and pernicious anemia; methylcobalamin is widely sold as a dietary supplement and is an approved pharmaceutical form in Japan (Rozebalamin, for ALS). In the body-recomp and longevity context, methylcobalamin is tracked for its roles in homocysteine reduction, myelin support, neurological recovery, and methylation cycle optimization — particularly in individuals on metformin or GLP-1/GIP receptor agonists, and those with MTHFR variants. Educational tracking entry only; not a prescription or dosing recommendation.
mechanism: Methylcobalamin serves as the active coenzyme for two key enzymatic reactions: (1) methionine synthase — catalyzing remethylation of homocysteine to methionine using 5-methyltetrahydrofolate as the methyl donor, directly supporting one-carbon methylation and regenerating tetrahydrofolate for nucleotide synthesis; and (2) methylmalonyl-CoA mutase (as adenosylcobalamin, a separate coenzyme form) — converting methylmalonyl-CoA to succinyl-CoA for odd-chain fatty acid oxidation and the TCA cycle. Methylcobalamin, being the already-methylated coenzyme form, does not require the intracellular demethylation and re-methylation steps that cyanocobalamin undergoes via the MMACHC enzyme system; however, current biochemical evidence indicates all cobalamin forms converge on the same [Co2+]Cbl intermediate intracellularly, making the superiority of methylcobalamin over cyanocobalamin for deficiency treatment unproven. Neurologically, high-dose methylcobalamin promotes Schwann cell differentiation, axon regeneration, increased Erk1/2 and Akt signaling, and BDNF secretion in preclinical models. Homocysteine lowering via the methionine synthase pathway reduces cardiovascular and neurotoxic risk associated with hyperhomocysteinemia.
monitoring_notes: Baseline serum B12 before initiating supplementation in at-risk populations; note that serum B12 alone is insensitive — methylmalonic acid (MMA) is the most sensitive functional marker and should be checked when serum B12 is 150-399 pg/mL
Serum potassium before and during initiation of B12 therapy in patients with known severe deficiency (rapid erythropoiesis risk)
Repeat B12 (and MMA if indicated) 2-3 months after initiating supplementation to confirm adequacy
Annual B12 screening for patients on long-term metformin, PPIs, or H2-receptor antagonists
Annual or biannual B12 screening for patients on GLP-1/GIP receptor agonists with concurrent metformin use or dietary restriction
Neurological examination at baseline and follow-up in patients with known deficiency — partial neurological recovery may occur over 6 weeks to 3 months; some damage may be irreversible if prolonged
Hematocrit, reticulocyte count, and platelet count during repletion of severe deficiency (reticulocytosis peaks at 5-7 days; thrombocytosis may develop)

Homocysteine and/or MMA as functional biomarkers in longevity/methylation optimization contexts — note that homocysteine has poor specificity for B12 deficiency alone (folate and B6 status also affect it)
LHON mutation carriers: screen regularly for B12 deficiency; manage any supplementation under specialist supervision
absolute_contraindications: Known hypersensitivity or anaphylaxis to cobalamin (vitamin B12) or cobalt

Leber hereditary optic neuropathy (LHON) — all forms of vitamin B12, including methylcobalamin, may precipitate rapid and severe optic nerve atrophy in affected individuals; LHON mutation carriers should undergo regular B12 screening and any supplementation should be managed only by a specialist
Confirmed anaphylaxis to cobalt-containing compounds
relative_contraindications: Renal impairment — parenteral formulations containing aluminum (cyanocobalamin injections) may accumulate aluminum at levels toxic to CNS and bone in patients with impaired kidney function (FDA label warning); methylcobalamin oral supplements do not carry this risk
Active polycythemia vera — correction of megaloblastic anemia with B12 may unmask or worsen polycythemia
Folate supplementation without confirmed B12 adequacy — high-dose folate in B12-deficient patients masks hematological signs while allowing irreversible neurological damage to progress

Hypokalemia — B12 therapy triggering rapid erythropoiesis can cause life-threatening hypokalemia; potassium levels must be monitored and corrected before and during initiation of B12 therapy in deficient patients
common_side_effects: Injection site pain, erythema, or induration (parenteral routes)
Mild diarrhea or gastrointestinal discomfort (high-dose oral)
Transient flushing or tingling sensations
Headache (uncommon)
Nausea
serious_adverse_events: Anaphylactic shock — rare but reported after parenteral B12 administration; epinephrine should be available in IM injection contexts
Severe hypokalemia — may occur during treatment of severe deficiency as rapid red cell production increases potassium demand; can be life-threatening if unmonitored
Optic nerve atrophy — in LHON patients, all cobalamin forms may precipitate rapid irreversible vision loss
Peripheral edema (rare, reported with high-dose parenteral B12)
Aluminum toxicity — specific to injectable cyanocobalamin formulations containing aluminum in renally impaired patients (not applicable to oral methylcobalamin supplements)

FDA-approved injectable forms are cyanocobalamin and hydroxocobalamin — not methylcobalamin specifically. Methylcobalamin is available in injectable form in Japan (Rozebalamin) and widely as oral supplements elsewhere. This dose describes the established pharmaceutical-class protocol and is documented for educational reference; it is not a prescription or dosing recommendation. Dosing must be individualized by a clinician.

dose_references.context: High-dose oral replacement for B12 deficiency including pernicious anemia (leverages passive absorption ~1-1.3% at supraphysiologic doses)

citation.title: Vitamin B12 — Health Professional Fact Sheet, NIH ODS
Oral vitamin B12 versus intramuscular vitamin B12 for vitamin B12 deficiency — PMC

dose_references.notes: Three RCTs (n=153 pooled in a 2018 Cochrane-derived analysis) found 1,000-2,000 mcg oral B12 daily similarly effective to IM injections for normalizing serum B12 levels. NIH ODS cites 1,000-2,000 mcg/day as the high-dose oral therapeutic range. Passive absorption (~1.2-1.3%) at this dose is sufficient to correct deficiency even in pernicious anemia with absent intrinsic factor. Educational reference for the documented clinical range — not a dosing recommendation.

dose_references.context: Methylcobalamin (mecobalamin) oral for peripheral neuropathy including diabetic peripheral neuropathy — standard clinical trial dose
dose_references.frequency: 1-3 times daily (total 500-1500 mcg/day) for 8-12 weeks or longer

citation.title: Efficacy and Safety of Mecobalamin on Peripheral Neuropathy: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials
Using corneal confocal microscopy to compare Mecobalamin intramuscular injections vs oral tablets in treating diabetic peripheral neuropathy: a RCT

dose_references.notes: Multiple RCTs in the 2020 meta-analysis (n=1,707 across 15 studies) evaluated mecobalamin for diabetic and herpetic neuropathy. A 1-year double-blind RCT (Didangelos 2021) administered 1 mg (1,000 mcg) oral methylcobalamin once daily for 12 months and found improvement in neurophysiology, pain scores, and quality of life. A corneal confocal microscopy RCT compared 0.5 mg IM 3x/week vs. 0.5 mg oral 3x/day (1.5 mg/day total) for 8 weeks. Combination mecobalamin showed stronger NCV benefit than monotherapy in the meta-analysis. Documented clinical-trial range, educational reference only — not a dosing recommendation.

dose_references.context: Ultra-high-dose methylcobalamin IM for early-stage ALS — JETALS Phase 3 RCT (Japan; compound approved in Japan as Rozebalamin 25mg injection in 2024)

citation.title: Efficacy and Safety of Ultrahigh-Dose Methylcobalamin in Early-Stage Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis: A Randomized Clinical Trial (JETALS)
ROZEBALAMIN for Injection 25mg (Mecobalamin) Approved in Japan for ALS — Eisai News Release

dose_references.notes: JETALS: multicenter, double-blind, placebo-controlled Phase 3 RCT in Japan (n=130, 25 centers, 2017-2019). 50 mg IM methylcobalamin twice weekly for 16 weeks significantly slowed ALSFRS-R decline by 43% vs. placebo (p=0.01). Rozebalamin 25mg IM received Japanese regulatory approval for ALS in 2024 (Eisai). NOT FDA-approved in the US. Dose is approximately 20,000x the physiologic RDA; NOT applicable to general supplementation or body recomposition contexts. Included for accurate evidence grading only — not a dosing recommendation.

dose_references.context: Methylcobalamin + methylfolate + pyridoxal-5-phosphate combination for homocysteine and LDL reduction in MTHFR polymorphism carriers
dose_references.frequency: Daily (2 capsules x 500 mcg MeCbl each, co-administered with 2 mg L-methylfolate and 100 mg P5P) for 180 days

citation.title: Effect of Methylfolate, Pyridoxal-5-Phosphate, and Methylcobalamin Supplementation on Homocysteine and LDL-C in Patients with MTHFR, MTR, and MTRR Polymorphisms

dose_references.notes: Randomized, double-blind, parallel-group trial (n=51 completers) in patients with MTHFR, MTR, MTRR polymorphisms. Total daily dose: 1,000 mcg methylcobalamin + 2 mg L-methylfolate + 100 mg pyridoxal-5-phosphate. Achieved 30% homocysteine reduction (p<0.01) and 7.5% LDL-C reduction vs. placebo over 180 days. Homozygous carriers showed 48.3% homocysteine reduction. Small single RCT; combination design means the methylcobalamin-specific contribution cannot be isolated. Educational reference only — not a dosing recommendation.

dose_references.context: Community and research-practice supplementation range — general health, body recomposition, longevity, and neuroprotection contexts
dose_references.notes: Community/research-practice convention, not clinically validated as a target range. In longevity, biohacking, and body-recomposition communities, oral and sublingual methylcobalamin is widely used at 500-5,000 mcg/day. At 500 mcg, passive absorption provides roughly 6-10 mcg absorbed. At 1,000-5,000 mcg, the absorbed fraction via passive diffusion (approximately 1-1.3%) provides 10-65 mcg. The 5,000 mcg ceiling reflects the upper end of commonly marketed supplement products. No upper tolerable intake level (UL) has been established by the FDA or NIH for B12 due to its low toxicity profile. This range is a community/research-practice convention, not clinically validated, and does not reflect a validated dose for any indication. Documented solely to surface the commonly cited range. Consult a clinician for individualized dosing.

synergies.rationale: Methylcobalamin and 5-methyltetrahydrofolate (5-MTHF) are the two direct substrates for the methionine synthase reaction: 5-MTHF donates its methyl group to cobalamin, which transfers it to homocysteine to generate methionine. In studies, combined methylcobalamin and L-methylfolate (the active folate form, bypassing MTHFR) lowered homocysteine more robustly than either alone, especially in MTHFR C677T and A1298C variant carriers. An RCT (n=51, 2024) using methylcobalamin 1,000 mcg + L-methylfolate 2 mg + P5P 100 mg daily for 180 days reduced homocysteine by 30% and LDL-C by 7.5% vs. placebo. Educational mechanism context only.
synergies.caution_notes: Folate supplementation in undiagnosed B12-deficient patients can mask hematological signs of B12 deficiency while allowing neurological damage to progress irreversibly. B12 status should be confirmed before or alongside high-dose folate. This is educational context — not a recommendation to self-supplement.

synergies.rationale: Vitamin B6 (specifically pyridoxal-5-phosphate, P5P) drives the transsulfuration pathway, converting homocysteine to cystathionine via cystathionine beta-synthase. This complements the remethylation pathway supported by B12 and folate, providing an alternative route for homocysteine clearance. The B6+B12+folate triad is the most-studied combination for hyperhomocysteinemia reduction in the literature. Meta-analyses of RCTs report B12 alone reduces homocysteine by ~7% beyond folic acid's ~25%, while combined B-vitamin supplementation is associated with 25-50% total reduction. Educational mechanism context only.
synergies.caution_notes: Despite effective homocysteine reduction, B-vitamin combination trials (HOPE-2, VISP, NORVIT) did not demonstrate significant reductions in hard cardiovascular outcomes — homocysteine lowering appears to be an incomplete surrogate for CV benefit. High-dose B6 (>200 mg/day long-term) carries risk of peripheral sensory neuropathy. Educational framing only, not a dosing recommendation.

synergies.rationale: Alpha-lipoic acid and methylcobalamin are frequently co-administered in neuropathy research, particularly diabetic neuropathy, due to complementary mechanisms: ALA acts as a potent antioxidant reducing oxidative stress in peripheral nerve tissue, while methylcobalamin supports axon regeneration and myelin formation via the methylation pathway. Post-marketing surveillance and open-label studies of fixed-dose combinations (methylcobalamin + ALA + folic acid + B6 + benfotiamine + biotin) reported reductions in neuropathy symptoms over 12 weeks. Evidence is primarily observational/open-label; controlled evidence for the combination specifically is limited. Educational context only.
synergies.caution_notes: Most combination neuropathy studies are industry-sponsored, open-label, and conducted without blinding. Combination products make it impossible to isolate methylcobalamin's individual contribution. Educational reference only — not a validated protocol.

synergies.rationale: Metformin reduces intestinal B12 absorption via interference with calcium-dependent intrinsic factor-B12 receptor binding in the ileum. Up to 30% of long-term metformin users develop B12 deficiency. This makes B12 status monitoring relevant in metformin users, particularly type 2 diabetes patients with concurrent diabetic peripheral neuropathy where B12 deficiency may worsen neuropathic symptoms independent of glycemic control. This is a monitoring relationship described for educational context, not a co-administration recommendation.
synergies.caution_notes: This is a monitoring-and-repletion relationship, not a pharmacodynamic synergy. Metformin is a drug requiring clinician oversight. Periodic B12 monitoring (annually or per clinician guidance) is commonly advised in metformin users. Educational context only — not a recommendation to self-supplement.

synergies.with_name: GLP-1/GIP Receptor Agonists (Semaglutide, Tirzepatide, Retatrutide) — context: indirect B12 monitoring
synergies.rationale: GLP-1 receptor agonists and dual GLP-1/GIP agonists substantially reduce caloric and protein intake, and when combined with concurrent metformin use (common in T2D), may further compound the risk of dietary B12 insufficiency. At-risk individuals (over age 50, vegetarian/vegan diet, metformin users) on these agents may warrant periodic B12 monitoring per clinician judgment. There is no evidence that these agents directly impair B12 absorption via a pharmacological mechanism distinct from reduced intake and metformin co-use. Educational monitoring context only.
synergies.caution_notes: IMPORTANT SAFETY NOTE: Eli Lilly issued a formal warning (2024) against compounded tirzepatide formulations that include vitamin B12, citing an identified chemical impurity from the combination with unknown safety, toxicity, and immunogenicity profile. Compounded GLP-1 peptides mixed with B12 in the same vial should be avoided. Oral B12 supplementation taken separately is a distinct matter. This is educational context — consult a prescribing clinician.

name: Glutathione
short_description: Glutathione (GSH) is a tripeptide (gamma-glutamyl-cysteinyl-glycine) and the body's master endogenous antioxidant — not a classic peptide hormone or growth factor. It exists in reduced (GSH) and oxidized (GSSG) forms and is found at high concentrations in virtually every human cell. Oral dietary-supplement forms are broadly sold and not FDA-approved as drugs; no injectable glutathione product holds FDA approval for any indication. Research interest spans oxidative stress, longevity, NAFLD, Parkinson's disease, and metabolic health.
mechanism: Glutathione is synthesized de novo from glutamate, cysteine (the rate-limiting precursor), and glycine via two ATP-dependent steps catalyzed by glutamate-cysteine ligase (GCL) and glutathione synthetase. Its antioxidant action proceeds primarily through glutathione peroxidases (GPx), which reduce hydrogen peroxide and lipid peroxides while oxidizing GSH to GSSG; glutathione reductase then regenerates GSH using NADPH. GSH also (1) conjugates electrophilic xenobiotics via glutathione-S-transferases for urinary/biliary excretion; (2) directly scavenges hydroxyl radicals and singlet oxygen; (3) regenerates vitamins C and E and alpha-lipoic acid within the broader antioxidant recycling network; (4) regulates immune function, including natural killer cell cytotoxicity; and (5) participates in leukotriene and prostaglandin synthesis. Cellular GSH declines with age — driven partly by impaired synthesis from lower cysteine and glycine availability — and this deficit is hypothesized to drive mitochondrial dysfunction, inflammation, and several aging-associated disease states. The liver synthesizes approximately 80-85% of circulating plasma GSH.
monitoring_notes: Liver function tests (ALT, AST, GGT) at baseline and periodically during IV glutathione therapy — LFT derangement reported in a controlled IV study
Renal function (creatinine, BUN) if using high-dose or prolonged IV therapy
Serum zinc levels with prolonged high-dose oral supplementation — GSH may chelate zinc
Oxidative stress biomarkers (e.g., 8-OHdG, GSH:GSSG ratio) may be used in research contexts to assess response; no standard clinical monitoring protocol established
Monitor blood glucose if combining with alpha-lipoic acid (ALA lowers blood glucose)
Confirm pharmaceutical-grade sterility and endotoxin testing certificates for any compounded injectable preparation — dietary-supplement-grade powder is not appropriate for sterile injection per FDA
Symptom monitoring for hypersensitivity reactions with IV administration, especially on first dose
absolute_contraindications: Active cytotoxic chemotherapy (platinum-based, alkylating agents) — GSH may antagonize the oxidative mechanism of cytotoxicity and promote treatment resistance; discuss with oncologist before any use
Known hypersensitivity to glutathione or any formulation component
Use of compounded injectable glutathione prepared from dietary-supplement-grade powder not meeting pharmaceutical sterility and endotoxin standards (FDA safety alert 2019)
relative_contraindications: Pregnancy and breastfeeding — insufficient safety data; avoid unless clinically indicated and supervised
Severe renal impairment — reduced capacity to clear oxidized metabolites; no established dosing adjustment data
Active malignancy (non-chemotherapy context) — theoretical concern that antioxidant supplementation could support tumor cell survival; discuss with oncologist
Concurrent immunosuppressant therapy — GSH supports immune activation (NK cell cytotoxicity); theoretical interaction with immunosuppression (e.g., post-transplant)
Nitrate medications (nitroglycerin) — mechanistic interaction via NO/thiol pathways; monitor cardiovascular status
G6PD deficiency — patients have impaired capacity to regenerate NADPH needed for GSH recycling; exogenous GSH supplementation effects are unpredictable
common_side_effects: Gastrointestinal upset (nausea, bloating, cramping) with oral supplementation — generally mild and dose-dependent
Skin rash or allergic reaction (rare, oral route)
Headache — reported occasionally with IV administration
Sinus irritation or eye puffiness with intranasal formulations (reported in Parkinson's trial)
Zinc depletion with prolonged high-dose oral supplementation (theoretical; GSH chelates zinc)
Loose stools or diarrhea at high oral doses
serious_adverse_events: Endotoxin-related systemic reactions with compounded IV glutathione: fever, chills, myalgia, headache, nausea, vomiting, hypotension, shock — FDA received reports of 7 adverse events from endotoxin-contaminated compounded product (2019); some samples tested at 5x the safe endotoxin limit
Hypersensitivity and anaphylaxis — at least one reported case with IV administration

Potential liver function test derangement — one controlled study (1200 mg IV 2x/week for 6 weeks) found 8/16 participants developed abnormal LFTs; a 2025 narrative review noted reports of liver failure following high-dose IV use
Potential increase in skin cancer susceptibility if used for skin lightening — shift from eumelanin (UV-protective brown pigment) to pheomelanin (red pigment) reduces UV protection

Possible reduction in chemotherapy efficacy — elevated tumor-cell GSH correlates with resistance to platinum-based agents (cisplatin) and alkylating agents; timing relative to active cancer treatment is critical

dose_references.context: Oral glutathione supplementation — 6-month RCT in healthy non-smoking adults (n=54): 250 mg/day (low dose) or 1000 mg/day (high dose). At 6 months, high-dose group showed 30-35% increase in erythrocyte, plasma, and lymphocyte GSH and 260% increase in buccal cells. Low-dose showed 17-29% increases in blood and erythrocytes. Natural killer cytotoxicity increased >2-fold in high-dose group vs placebo at 3 months. Effects were dose- and time-dependent and reversed after discontinuation.

dose_references.notes: Richie JP et al., Eur J Nutr 2015 (PMID 24791752). First RCT demonstrating oral GSH effectively raises body compartment stores. 6-month duration; healthy adults only; no clinical endpoints assessed beyond biomarkers. Educational reference only — not a dosing recommendation.

dose_references.context: Oral glutathione for NAFLD — multicenter open-label pilot study (n=34 NAFLD patients). After 3-month lifestyle intervention, patients received 300 mg/day oral L-glutathione for 4 months. Primary outcome ALT significantly reduced; triglycerides, NEFA, and ferritin also decreased. Significant limitations: no control group, single arm.

dose_references.notes: Honda Y et al., BMC Gastroenterol 2017 (PMC5549431). Open-label, uncontrolled pilot study. Consistent with 2025 literature review (PMC11940638) finding the same 300 mg/day dose across three Asian NAFLD trials totaling n=109. Not sufficient for clinical dosing recommendation without confirmatory RCT.

dose_references.context: Intranasal glutathione for Parkinson's disease — Phase IIb double-blind placebo-controlled pilot trial (n=45, Hoehn-Yahr 1-3). Three arms: placebo, 100 mg GSH, or 200 mg GSH three times daily for 12 weeks via mucosal atomization device. All cohorts including placebo improved; neither active arm showed superiority over placebo on primary UPDRS endpoints. High-dose arm showed statistically significant within-group improvement only.

citation.title: Phase IIb Study of Intranasal Glutathione in Parkinson's Disease

dose_references.notes: Mischley LK et al., Parkinson's Disease 2017 (PMC5438472). Downgraded to HUMAN_OBS: small Phase IIb pilot in which no active arm showed superiority vs placebo on primary endpoints, so efficacy is not established. Researchers recommended delayed-start design for future trials. Intranasal route bypasses blood-brain barrier; CNS uptake confirmed by imaging in a separate phase I/IIa study (PMC5516583). Educational reference only — not a dosing recommendation.

dose_references.context: GlyNAC (glutathione precursors glycine + N-acetylcysteine) supplementation in older adults — 16-week double-blind placebo-controlled RCT (n=24 older adults aged 61-80). Dose: 100 mg/kg/day each of glycine and NAC (GlyNAC arm) vs isonitrogenous alanine placebo. Outcomes: muscle glutathione +121-164%, RBC GSH +173-225%, oxidative stress markers reduced ~70%, improvements in gait speed, grip strength, mitochondrial fatty-acid oxidation, insulin resistance, and inflammation. No adverse events reported.
dose_references.frequency: once daily (each of glycine and NAC)

citation.title: Supplementing Glycine and N-Acetylcysteine (GlyNAC) in Older Adults Improves Glutathione Deficiency, Oxidative Stress, Mitochondrial Dysfunction, Inflammation, Physical Function, and Aging Hallmarks: A Randomized Clinical Trial
citation.source: Oxford Academic / Journals of Gerontology Series A

dose_references.notes: Kumar P et al., Journals of Gerontology Series A 2023 (PMID 35975308; Oxford Academic). GlyNAC supplies precursors (cysteine via NAC and glycine) to restore GSH synthesis rather than delivering exogenous GSH. Small single-center trial (n=24); participants had elevated BMI and triglycerides, limiting generalizability. Indirect evidence for the GSH-restoration strategy. Educational reference only — not a dosing recommendation.

dose_references.context: COMMUNITY / RESEARCH-PRACTICE dosing range — widely cited across wellness, IV-drip, and longevity-medicine contexts but NOT derived from controlled clinical trials establishing efficacy or safety at these ranges. Oral: 250-1000 mg/day; IV/injection: 600-1400 mg per session, 2-3 times per week. Liposomal oral formulations often started at 100-250 mg/day titrating to 500 mg/day. IV push typically given over 3-5 minutes. These ranges appear consistently across compounding pharmacy guidance, IV-drip clinic protocols, and wellness review articles.

citation.title: FDA highlights concerns with using dietary ingredient glutathione to compound sterile injectables

dose_references.notes: This row reflects community/research-practice convention, not clinically validated dosing — summarized from wellness protocol aggregators and compounding pharmacy guidance. IV glutathione is not FDA-approved; compounded injectable glutathione carries contamination risks flagged by FDA (2019 endotoxin alert). Oral ranges partially overlap with the Richie 2015 RCT (250-1000 mg/day) but community IV dosing has no robust RCT support. Do not interpret as a clinical recommendation. Always consult a licensed clinician.

synergies.rationale: NAC is the most evidence-backed glutathione precursor strategy. NAC delivers cysteine (the rate-limiting substrate for GSH synthesis) intracellularly where it can be incorporated into GSH via GCL and glutathione synthetase. This is the mechanistic basis of GlyNAC (glycine + NAC) supplementation, which in a 16-week RCT raised muscle GSH >120% and RBC GSH >170% in older adults with metabolic stress. NAC is also FDA-approved as an acetaminophen antidote specifically because it replenishes hepatic glutathione.
synergies.caution_notes: NAC in high doses may cause GI upset, and at very high IV doses has caused anaphylactoid reactions. Do not combine with chemotherapy without oncologist guidance — GSH elevation may reduce cytotoxic efficacy of alkylating agents. Educational context only; any combination should be supervised by a licensed clinician.

synergies.rationale: ALA participates in the antioxidant recycling network: it regenerates vitamins C and E and, via lipoamide dehydrogenase, can support regeneration of GSH from GSSG. A human study in physically active males (PMID 19093729) and a Journal of Applied Physiology study (1999) reported ALA supplementation supporting tissue glutathione homeostasis at rest and after exercise. The mechanistic rationale — complementary redox cycling and shared protection of mitochondrial function — is well-established in vitro and in animal models.
synergies.caution_notes: ALA may lower blood glucose; monitor in diabetics. High-dose ALA (>600 mg/day) has been associated with thiamine depletion with prolonged use. Combinatorial human RCTs specifically for ALA + GSH remain limited. Educational context only.

synergies.rationale: Vitamin C and glutathione are closely linked in the antioxidant recycling network: GSH reduces dehydroascorbic acid back to ascorbic acid, regenerating active vitamin C, while ascorbic acid can spare GSH by directly quenching certain reactive oxygen species. The relationship is bidirectional and mechanistically well-established. The two are also frequently co-administered together in IV-drip wellness protocols, though such combinations lack controlled efficacy evidence.
synergies.caution_notes: High-dose IV vitamin C is contraindicated in G6PD deficiency and may cause oxalate nephropathy. Combination IV protocols lack controlled efficacy evidence. Educational context only — not a protocol recommendation.

synergies.rationale: Glycine is the third amino acid in GSH synthesis and, together with cysteine, is often deficient in older adults — impairing de novo GSH production even when cysteine is available. Supplying glycine alongside NAC (the GlyNAC strategy) is the mechanistic basis of the Kumar et al. 2023 RCT showing robust GSH restoration in aging. Glycine alone has also been studied for anti-inflammatory and sleep-related properties, suggesting potential complementary relevance in longevity contexts.
synergies.caution_notes: Glycine is generally well tolerated at supplemental doses. The GlyNAC RCT used weight-based dosing; individual needs vary and any regimen should be individualized with a clinician. Evidence is from a single small single-center trial. Educational context only.

synergies.rationale: Silymarin (the active complex from milk thistle) has been reported to increase hepatic GSH levels in animal models, with some human observational data in liver disease. Both act through hepatoprotective antioxidant pathways, and silymarin may upregulate GCL activity. The combination is commonly discussed in NAFLD and hepatoprotection contexts.
synergies.caution_notes: Human RCT evidence for this specific combination is lacking. Silymarin may affect CYP3A4 and CYP2C9 enzyme activity; clinician review is advised if a patient takes medications metabolized by these pathways. Educational context only.

name: hCG (Human Chorionic Gonadotropin)
short_description: hCG is a glycoprotein hormone — not a classic peptide — produced naturally during pregnancy and extracted from human urine (Pregnyl, Novarel) or produced via recombinant technology (Ovidrel/choriogonadotropin alfa). It functions as a luteinizing hormone (LH) analog, stimulating Leydig cells in the testes to produce testosterone and intratesticular testosterone (ITT). FDA-approved for prepubertal cryptorchidism, hypogonadotropic hypogonadism in males, and ovulation induction in females. Discussed in body-recomposition contexts for off-label use to preserve testicular function and spermatogenesis during exogenous testosterone therapy or to support HPT-axis recovery following suppression. Explicitly not FDA-approved for weight loss; OTC homeopathic hCG diet products are illegal and have been subject to FDA/FTC enforcement action. Compounding access for hCG was severely restricted after its 2020 reclassification as a biologic under the BPCI Act. Educational reference only — not a prescription or protocol.
mechanism: hCG shares structural and functional homology with pituitary LH and binds the same LH/hCG receptor (LHCGR) on testicular Leydig cells, stimulating cAMP production and downstream testosterone biosynthesis. This raises both serum testosterone and intratesticular testosterone (ITT) — the latter being the key driver of spermatogenesis. hCG also carries minor FSH-like activity. Because it bypasses the hypothalamic-pituitary axis and acts directly on the gonad, it can restore or maintain Leydig cell function even when endogenous LH is suppressed by exogenous androgens. At high or sustained doses, Leydig cell desensitization via LH receptor downregulation has been documented in animal models and described clinically, providing a mechanistic rationale for the clinical preference for lower, more frequent dosing rather than large infrequent boluses.
monitoring_notes: Baseline: serum total testosterone, LH, FSH, estradiol, PSA (males), hematocrit/hemoglobin, liver function
Clinician follow-up of testosterone after initiation and periodically thereafter; male hypogonadism targets are individualized by the prescribing clinician
Monitor estradiol; elevated estradiol or an unfavorable T:E2 ratio may prompt the prescribing clinician to reassess therapy
Monitor hematocrit periodically; hCG monotherapy data show lower erythrocytosis risk vs. exogenous testosterone, but polycythemia risk still exists
PSA monitoring in males per AUA testosterone deficiency guidelines (baseline and follow-up)
Semen analysis if fertility preservation/restoration is the goal

For females undergoing ovulation induction: ultrasound monitoring for follicular response and ovarian enlargement; watch for OHSS symptoms (rapid weight gain, abdominal distension, severe pelvic pain) — urgent evaluation if these develop
Patients on concurrent aromatase inhibitors: monitor estradiol closely to avoid over-suppression

Regulatory context: since March 2020 (BPCI Act), hCG is classified as a biologic; compounded hCG is severely restricted. Only FDA-approved branded products (Pregnyl, Novarel, Ovidrel) or a rare licensed 503B outsourcing facility may legally provide it.
This entry is educational and tracks user- or clinician-supplied protocols; it is not medical advice and does not prescribe.
absolute_contraindications: Known or suspected hormone-sensitive malignancies (prostate cancer, androgen-sensitive tumors, hypothalamic or pituitary tumors)
Primary ovarian failure (high FSH) — hCG cannot overcome this
Hypersensitivity or anaphylaxis to gonadotropins or excipients in approved preparations
Uncontrolled thyroid or adrenal disorders (correct prior to use)
Precocious puberty (hCG can accelerate/worsen it)
Reproductive tract malformations incompatible with pregnancy (females)
Abnormal uterine bleeding of undetermined cause
Ovarian cysts or enlargement not due to polycystic ovary syndrome (females)

Personal history of thromboembolic disease (DVT, pulmonary embolism, ischemic stroke) — OHSS-associated thromboembolism is a labeled serious adverse event for ovulation-induction doses; prior thromboembolic event contraindicates high-dose hCG without specialist supervision.
relative_contraindications: Elevated PSA or untreated benign prostatic hypertrophy (hCG raises testosterone, which may worsen symptoms)
Polycythemia or hematocrit >52% (testosterone-elevating effect may raise hematocrit further, though hCG monotherapy data showed lower erythrocytosis risk vs. direct TRT)
History of testicular maldescent with prior surgery (may alter response)
Active epilepsy or migraine (androgen-related fluid retention may exacerbate)
Obesity with high aromatization potential (elevated estradiol conversion risk; monitor estradiol closely)
common_side_effects: Injection site reactions: pain, bruising, redness, swelling
Gynecomastia in males (driven by testosterone-to-estradiol aromatization)
Water and sodium retention / edema
Headache, irritability, restlessness, fatigue, depression
Elevated estradiol in males (can cause mood changes, libido changes)
Ovarian enlargement and/or cysts in females
Breast pain / tenderness in females
Nausea and gastrointestinal upset (especially in ART context)
Weight gain (fluid-related)
serious_adverse_events: Ovarian Hyperstimulation Syndrome (OHSS): can be life-threatening; severe OHSS associated with renal failure, hypovolemic shock, acute respiratory distress, and thromboembolic events
Thromboembolic events: deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism, cerebral thrombosis — particularly in female ART context with OHSS
Anaphylaxis/hypersensitivity reactions (reported with urinary-derived preparations)
Precocious puberty in pediatric patients treated for cryptorchidism (requires treatment discontinuation if signs appear)
Ovarian torsion (females)
Multiple gestations / increased miscarriage risk (females)
Ectopic pregnancy (elevated risk in ART context)
Leydig cell desensitization / receptor downregulation with prolonged high-dose use (documented in animal models; described clinically; may paradoxically reduce testosterone production)
Potential exacerbation of hormone-sensitive malignancies (particularly prostate cancer) — hCG-driven testosterone elevation contraindicated in this context

This range reflects ranges widely circulated in anabolic-steroid user communities for testicular maintenance during cycles and HPTA recovery (so-called 'PCT'). No primary RCT validates this specific use case. High-dose or prolonged use may cause Leydig cell desensitization via LH receptor downregulation. The one anecdotal citation below describes PCT outcomes generally and does not validate any specific dose.

synergies.rationale: hCG stimulates Leydig cells to produce testosterone (LH-axis) but does not directly stimulate Sertoli cells, which require FSH for full spermatogenesis. In clinically diagnosed hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, combined hCG plus FSH (or long-acting corifollitropin alfa) is a described clinical approach; a single-arm open-label phase III trial reported 77.8% of men reaching sperm count ≥1×10^6/mL. This is a complementary, not redundant, biological pairing — presented for education only.
synergies.caution_notes: Downgraded to HUMAN_OBS because the supporting trial was single-arm open-label, not randomized. FSH-containing products are prescription-only and subject to strict FDA/compounding regulation. Combined use is studied for clinically diagnosed hypogonadotropic hypogonadism — not general performance use. Risk of multi-follicular response in female patients. Any combination should be directed and monitored by a prescribing clinician.

synergies.rationale: Exogenous testosterone suppresses endogenous LH/FSH, leading to Leydig cell atrophy, reduced ITT, and azoospermia in a large fraction of men on TRT. Concurrent low-dose hCG can maintain Leydig cell stimulation and ITT, which has been associated with preserved spermatogenesis during ongoing TRT in retrospective human data (no patient became azoospermic in the cited cohort). Presented to explain the biological interaction, not to recommend a regimen.
synergies.caution_notes: This combination increases estradiol via aromatization of both exogenous and endogenous testosterone; gynecomastia risk is elevated. Estradiol monitoring is warranted. It does not fully replace FSH when complete spermatogenesis restoration is the goal. Dose, frequency, and whether to combine at all are clinical decisions for a prescribing clinician — this entry does not specify a dose.

synergies.rationale: Clomiphene is a SERM that blocks hypothalamic estrogen receptors, increasing endogenous GnRH, LH, and FSH release — biologically complementary to hCG's direct Leydig-cell stimulation. In post-testosterone-cycle recovery and hypogonadism management, combined hCG plus SERM regimens have been associated with semen-parameter recovery in observational data (one 2015 review reported return of spermatogenesis in ~95.9% of cases with hCG plus clomiphene/tamoxifen combinations). Educational rationale only.
synergies.caution_notes: Clomiphene is off-label for male hypogonadism/fertility in the US and has mood/vision side effects. hCG and clomiphene can both elevate estradiol — estradiol monitoring and gynecomastia vigilance are warranted. Timing of agents materially affects HPTA recovery and is a clinician-managed decision. This entry does not specify doses or sequencing.

synergies.rationale: hCG-driven testosterone elevation increases estradiol via peripheral aromatization. In clinical practice an aromatase inhibitor is sometimes used to manage estradiol in men prone to gynecomastia, water retention, or mood symptoms during hCG therapy; reduced estradiol feedback can also raise testosterone. Framed as a clinician-managed monitoring strategy for education, not a self-administered protocol.
synergies.caution_notes: Over-suppression of estradiol (below roughly 20 pg/mL in men) is associated with bone loss, adverse lipid changes, sexual dysfunction, and joint pain. AI use with hCG requires careful estradiol monitoring by a clinician. AIs are prescription medications with their own interaction profiles. This entry does not recommend initiating an AI or any dose.

name: BPC-157 (Body Protection Compound-157)
short_description: BPC-157 is a synthetic 15-amino-acid peptide (pentadecapeptide) derived from a sequence found in human gastric juice. It is NOT FDA-approved and is classified as an unapproved drug. BPC-157 is prohibited by WADA under the S0 Non-Approved Substances category. The overwhelming majority of evidence derives from rodent and other animal models; human data is extremely limited and consists only of small, non-controlled pilot studies. It is not available through any licensed compounding pharmacy for patient use in the United States as of 2025 (FDA Category 2 designation in September 2023; FDA removed it from Category 2 in April 2026 pending PCAC review). The compound is widely discussed in research and athletic communities as a tissue-repair and cytoprotection agent, but these uses remain investigational and clinically unvalidated.
mechanism: BPC-157 is proposed to operate through multiple complementary pathways. Its primary documented mechanism involves modulation of the nitric oxide (NO) system — it activates eNOS and upregulates antioxidant enzymes (including heme oxygenase HO-1) while simultaneously exhibiting bidirectional NO regulation that limits cytotoxic NO activity. Key signaling involves the VEGFR2-Akt-eNOS and Src-Caveolin-1-eNOS pathways, driving angiogenesis and endothelial tube formation. BPC-157 also activates the FAK-paxillin pathway, promoting fibroblast migration and collagen synthesis. It enhances growth hormone receptor expression in tendon fibroblasts (via JAK-STAT pathway sensitization), promotes gene expression cascades within minutes of tissue injury (Akt1, Grb2, Nos3, Pik3cd, Src, Ptk2), and exerts anti-inflammatory effects by modulating pro-inflammatory cytokines. Gastrointestinal cytoprotection appears prostaglandin-independent, working through direct mucosal repair. The peptide is stable in human gastric acid for over 24 hours, enabling oral bioavailability in preclinical models. Pharmacokinetic data (rat/dog) shows peak concentration within 3-8 minutes of parenteral administration and an elimination half-life under 30 minutes.
monitoring_notes: BPC-157 has no established therapeutic drug monitoring protocol — no validated assay for serum levels in clinical use
If used in any investigational or off-label context, a clinician should monitor for injection site reactions and systemic inflammatory signs
Cancer screening history should be reviewed and discussed with an oncologist before any use given unresolved VEGFR2-mediated tumor growth concern
Liver function monitoring may be prudent given hepatic metabolism and the theoretical proline oxidase metabolite pathway
Complete blood count and inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR) as baseline given immunogenicity concerns raised by FDA
Regulatory status is actively evolving — clinicians should verify current FDA compounding status before any patient discussion
absolute_contraindications: Active or suspected malignancy (theoretical angiogenic tumor-promotion risk via VEGFR2; no human data to quantify but unresolved theoretical concern)
Pregnancy and breastfeeding (no human safety data; embryo-fetal toxicity studies are preclinical only)
Pediatric use (no data)
Competitive athletes subject to WADA-compliant anti-doping rules (prohibited S0 substance, no TUE available, sanctions documented)
relative_contraindications: History of malignancy (unresolved angiogenesis concern)
Concurrent use of anticoagulants or antiplatelet agents (NO system enhancement may augment anticoagulant effects — animal data only)
Cardiovascular disease with hemodynamic instability (NO system modulation)
Immunocompromised states (immunogenicity risk uncharacterized)
Patients sourcing from non-pharmaceutical-grade suppliers (impurity and dose accuracy risk)
common_side_effects: Injection site pain (documented in animal and limited human use)
Injection site irritation or mild necrosis (noted in preclinical literature when injected as aqueous solution)
No comprehensive human adverse-event dataset exists — the above represents the available documented reports
serious_adverse_events: Theoretical tumor promotion via VEGFR2-mediated angiogenesis — VEGF/VEGFR2 pathways are active in approximately 50% of human cancers; this risk is debated in the literature and has not been confirmed in animal carcinogenicity studies, but has not been ruled out in humans
Potential for iron accumulation and heme metabolism disruption via excessive NO pathway activation (theoretical, based on mechanistic review)
Immunogenicity / immune reactions — cited by FDA as a rationale for its 2023 Category 2 compounding restriction
Manufacturing impurity risk from non-pharmaceutical-grade sourcing (black market / research chemical supply)
Unknown long-term safety profile — no chronic human exposure data exists

citation.title: Multifunctionality and Possible Medical Application of the BPC 157 Peptide — Literature and Patent Review

dose_references.notes: This row reflects a community/research-practice convention, not clinically validated. It is the widely-documented community and research-practice convention for BPC-157 subcutaneous or intramuscular use, typically cited as 200-500 mcg once or twice daily. This range is derived from extrapolation of animal-model doses and community experience — it is NOT based on any completed human dose-finding clinical trial and is community/research-practice convention, not clinically validated. It is included strictly as a reference range; no clinical or regulatory body endorses this dosing. Oral use is sometimes cited at higher amounts (1-5 mg/day) due to assumed lower bioavailability, but this is similarly unvalidated in humans. Do NOT present to users as a recommended dose.

synergies.rationale: BPC-157 and TB-500 are described in the literature as mechanistically complementary in the tissue repair cascade. BPC-157 promotes angiogenesis (via VEGFR2-Akt-eNOS) and fibroblast activity, while TB-500 upregulates actin polymerization and cell migration (via the Tβ4-actin pathway), promoting stem cell mobilization and reducing fibrotic scarring. They are theorized to address different phases of healing: BPC-157 primarily during proliferation (fibroblast and collagen) and TB-500 during inflammation control and remodeling. Evidence for the combination is MECHANISTIC and community-observational only — no controlled human trial has evaluated the combined stack. This is educational context, not a protocol recommendation.
synergies.caution_notes: No controlled human data exists for this combination. Both compounds are individually unapproved for human use. Additive angiogenic effects are unstudied and could theoretically compound tumor-related angiogenesis concerns. Not a clinically validated protocol. Discuss any combination with a qualified clinician.

synergies.rationale: GHK-Cu is reported to promote collagen and glycosaminoglycan synthesis and to upregulate antioxidant enzymes (superoxide dismutase, catalase). BPC-157 is reported to provide vascular support and anti-inflammatory signaling. The theoretical rationale discussed in the literature is 'vascular scaffolding plus matrix construction': BPC-157 supporting blood supply and inflammatory modulation, GHK-Cu supporting collagen deposition and extracellular matrix quality. Evidence is MECHANISTIC — no co-administration human study exists. Presented for educational understanding only.
synergies.caution_notes: Purely mechanistic rationale. No human or animal co-administration study identified. Both are unapproved for human use. Combination effects are unknown. Not a validated protocol.

synergies.rationale: KPV is a tripeptide alpha-MSH fragment with documented NF-kB inhibition and anti-inflammatory activity in gut models. BPC-157 has reported cytoprotective and NO-modulating effects on gastrointestinal mucosa. The theoretical rationale described in the literature is complementary anti-inflammatory pathways in GI tissue repair — KPV reducing upstream cytokine signaling while BPC-157 supports mucosal integrity and angiogenesis. Evidence is MECHANISTIC; no direct combination trial exists. Educational context only.
synergies.caution_notes: No combination human data. Both compounds are unapproved for human use. Mechanistic rationale is plausible but unvalidated in any controlled study. Not a clinically validated protocol.

name: TB-500 (Thymosin Beta-4 Fragment 17–23)
short_description: TB-500 is a synthetic 7-amino-acid fragment (residues 17–23, sequence Ac-LKKTETQ) derived from the full 43-amino-acid endogenous peptide Thymosin Beta-4 (Tβ4). It is NOT FDA-approved for any human indication. The full-length Tβ4 has been investigated in early-phase human trials; the isolated TB-500 fragment lacks any dedicated human clinical trials as of 2026, so no human dose for the fragment has been established. Research interest centers on tissue repair, angiogenesis, anti-inflammation, and anti-fibrosis. It is banned by WADA at all times as a growth factor modulator. Educational reference only; no clinically validated human dosing protocol for the fragment exists.
mechanism: TB-500 contains the primary actin-sequestering domain of full-length Thymosin Beta-4. Proposed actions (largely from preclinical/in-vitro work on Tβ4): (1) G-actin sequestration, reorganizing the cytoskeleton to promote cell migration and wound closure; (2) promotion of angiogenesis via PI3K/Akt/eNOS signaling in endothelial progenitor cells; (3) anti-inflammatory effects through NF-κB pathway inhibition; (4) anti-fibrotic activity by suppressing the TGF-β/Smad axis; (5) anti-apoptotic effects via upregulation of BCL-2 and reduction of the Bax/BCL-2 ratio; and (6) modulation of Wnt/β-catenin signaling supporting stem/progenitor cell maturation. The full-length Tβ4 contains additional functional domains absent in the TB-500 fragment, so findings for full-length Tβ4 do not directly transfer to the fragment. Sources: PMC8724243 (Frontiers in Endocrinology 2022); PMC8419156 (CPT Pharmacometrics 2021); Bjørklund et al., Curr Med Chem 2020 (PMID 31333080); Sosne et al., J Investig Dermatol 1999 (PMID 10469335).
monitoring_notes: Cancer screening (history, physical, appropriate labs/imaging) before any use of a pro-angiogenic peptide, particularly in individuals aged 40+ or with cancer risk factors
Monitor injection sites for signs of infection, excessive inflammation, or induration
Baseline liver and renal function (CMP/BMP) given the lack of PK data in hepatic/renal impairment
Assess for emerging lymphadenopathy or unexpected tissue growths during use — report immediately to a treating clinician
WADA-prohibited: do not use if a competitive athlete subject to anti-doping jurisdiction
Any use should occur under clinical supervision with explicit informed consent documenting research-only status and absence of FDA approval
Document baseline inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR) if tracking a specific injury indication, to monitor response and avoid masking underlying pathology
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy of any type (pro-angiogenic mechanism may theoretically support tumor vasculature)
Pregnancy (no safety data; effects on embryogenesis unknown)
Lactation (no safety data)
Known hypersensitivity to thymosin beta-4 or any component of the formulation
relative_contraindications: Personal history of cancer within the past 5 years (discuss with an oncologist before any use)
Uncontrolled autoimmune disease (immune-modulating effects of unknown net direction in active autoimmune states)
Severe hepatic or renal impairment (pharmacokinetics uncharacterized)
Bleeding disorders or concurrent anticoagulant therapy (injections carry bleeding risk)
Athletes subject to WADA or USADA testing — TB-500 and Tβ4 are prohibited at all times as growth factor modulators (non-Specified Substance)
Active or recent serious infection (pro-angiogenic and immune-modulatory effects in infected tissue not characterized)
common_side_effects: Injection site pain, redness, or swelling (most commonly reported anecdotally)
Mild transient fatigue (reported anecdotally)
Mild headache (noted in some full-length Tβ4 early-phase subjects)
Transient mood changes (anecdotal, unconfirmed)
Mild, self-resolving abnormal blood biochemistry values (observed in full-length Tβ4 Phase I trial)
serious_adverse_events: Theoretical tumor promotion or acceleration in individuals with occult or active malignancy via pro-angiogenic mechanism — no confirmed human cases but mechanistically plausible (Tβ4 overexpression documented in pancreatic, lung, head-and-neck, and renal cancers in vitro; PMC2930015)

Unknown long-term immunogenicity risk with repeated injection of a non-endogenous fragment preparation — full-length endogenous Tβ4 showed minimal anti-drug antibodies in Phase I, but TB-500 fragment-specific data is absent
Risks from unregulated compounding: contamination, endotoxin, incorrect peptide sequence, or incorrect concentration — TB-500 is sold only as a research chemical in most jurisdictions
No long-term human cardiac or systemic safety monitoring data exists for systemic TB-500 fragment use

citation.title: TB-500 Background, Indications, Efficacy, and Safety — clinical reference

dose_references.notes: This row captures a community/research-practice convention, not clinically validated. A loading total of roughly 4–7.66 mg/week (split over 2–3 subcutaneous injections) followed by a maintenance phase of ~2–2.5 mg/week is the most commonly cited convention in research forums and practitioner summaries. These figures are NOT derived from any human RCT or FDA-approved protocol, are NOT clinically validated, and represent extrapolation from animal studies and anecdotal practitioner use. TB-500 is a research compound not approved for human administration. Included solely as a reference for users who may have received such a protocol from a clinician or researcher; it carries no endorsement and is not a recommendation.

synergies.rationale: BPC-157 is the compound most commonly discussed alongside TB-500 in research and practitioner literature. The proposed mechanistic rationale is complementary and non-overlapping: BPC-157 is associated with local growth-factor and nitric-oxide signaling for tendon, ligament, and gut tissue, while Tβ4/TB-500 is associated with actin remodeling, angiogenesis, and anti-fibrosis acting more broadly. Educationally, the two are described as covering both local repair signaling and broader cell migration. No dedicated human RCT has tested the combination; the rationale is mechanistic and based on separate preclinical evidence for each compound. This pairing (the 'GLOW' concept with GHK-Cu) is widely cited in research-practice contexts but is not a validated protocol.
synergies.caution_notes: Neither compound is FDA-approved for systemic use. Combining two unapproved peptides increases uncertainty around unknown interaction profiles, cumulative immunogenicity risk, and dual pro-angiogenic activity (theoretical tumor-vasculature concern). Educational reference only — not a recommended protocol. Discuss any combination with a qualified clinician.

synergies.rationale: GHK-Cu (copper tripeptide) is associated with collagen synthesis, decorin upregulation, and lysyl-oxidase-mediated collagen cross-linking, while Tβ4/TB-500 is associated with anti-fibrotic activity via TGF-β/Smad inhibition. Educationally, the pairing is described as potentially supporting organized collagen deposition (GHK-Cu for collagen quantity/quality, Tβ4 for limiting pathological fibrosis), with both modulating matrix metalloproteinases. This combination appears in the 'GLOW' concept (BPC-157 + TB-500 + GHK-Cu) discussed in research-practice literature. Evidence is mechanistic only; no human RCT data for the combination exists.
synergies.caution_notes: GHK-Cu is also unapproved for systemic injection in humans. The stack rationale is theoretical. Educational reference only — not a recommended protocol. Discuss any combination with a qualified clinician.

name: KPV (Lys-Pro-Val)
short_description: KPV is a synthetic tripeptide (Lys-Pro-Val) corresponding to residues 11-13 of alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone (alpha-MSH). It retains the anti-inflammatory properties of the parent neuropeptide without its pigmentary or melanocortin-receptor-mediated hormonal effects. KPV is a research compound — NOT FDA-approved for any indication — with a preclinical evidence base spanning intestinal inflammation, skin inflammation, wound healing, and antimicrobial models. No completed human clinical trials have been published as of mid-2026. It is under FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) review for potential inclusion on the 503A Bulk Drug Substances list (meeting scheduled July 2026).
mechanism: KPV exerts anti-inflammatory effects through at least two distinct, receptor-independent pathways. First, in intestinal epithelial and immune cells, KPV is actively transported intracellularly via PepT1 (the intestinal di/tripeptide transporter SLC15A1), which is upregulated during intestinal inflammation — making oral delivery particularly relevant in active colitis. Once intracellular, KPV stabilizes IkappaB-alpha, thereby suppressing nuclear translocation of NF-kappaB p65 (RelA) and downstream expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6, IL-8, IL-12). Second, in airway and other epithelial cells, KPV translocates directly to the nucleus where it competitively blocks the importin-alpha3 / p65RelA interaction, preventing NF-kappaB nuclear import independently of receptor signaling. KPV also suppresses MAP kinase (MAPK) inflammatory cascades, reduces neutrophil infiltration, and promotes IL-10 production. Unlike full-length alpha-MSH, KPV does not measurably elevate intracellular cAMP and has no clinically meaningful effect on pigmentation or the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis at studied concentrations. KPV additionally demonstrates direct antimicrobial activity against Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA) and Candida albicans through membrane disruption mechanisms, though concentrations required for direct microbicidal activity are higher than those needed for anti-inflammatory effects.
monitoring_notes: No validated human monitoring protocol exists — the following reflect general principles for investigational peptide use and are not a treatment recommendation
Baseline: document inflammatory symptoms, any active infections, autoimmune history, current medications, hepatic and renal function
During use: monitor for local injection-site reactions; assess GI tolerance with oral formulations

Periodic: if used long-term (beyond 4-8 weeks), a clinician may consider CBC and CMP to screen for unexpected hematologic or hepatic changes — no specific KPV-driven abnormalities have been identified in animal studies, but long-term human data is absent
Immunocompromised individuals: extra vigilance for signs of opportunistic infection given theoretical immune-modulating effects
Discontinue and consult a clinician if systemic symptoms (fever, rash, lymphadenopathy, unusual fatigue) develop
No therapeutic drug monitoring assays exist for KPV
absolute_contraindications: Known hypersensitivity or allergic reaction to KPV or any component of its formulation
Pregnancy — no reproductive or developmental safety data exists in any species for KPV specifically
Active severe systemic infection where the immune-inflammatory response is critical to survival (e.g., sepsis) — based on mechanistic concern, not direct evidence
relative_contraindications: Breastfeeding — no lactation safety data
Severe immunocompromise (organ transplant on immunosuppressants, active hematologic malignancy, CD4 count below 200 in HIV) — theoretical additive immunosuppression, unstudied
Concurrent use of prescribed systemic immunomodulators (corticosteroids, TNF inhibitors, JAK inhibitors) — potential additive immunosuppressive effect, drug-drug interaction unstudied
Active autoimmune disease under specialist management — modification of NF-kappaB signaling without specialist coordination could complicate disease management
Active IBD managed with approved therapies — combination with unvalidated agents not studied; do not substitute for established therapy
History of melanoma or other melanocortin-receptor-expressing tumors — highly theoretical based on parent peptide biology; KPV has minimal MC1R binding but caution is warranted pending more data
common_side_effects: Injection-site erythema or mild induration (subcutaneous route) — most frequently reported local reaction in research-practice use
Transient mild nausea or GI discomfort at higher oral doses — likely related to local PepT1 saturation
Mild headache in the first days of use — reported anecdotally, mechanism unclear
Potential histamine sensitivity reactions in individuals with mast cell activation syndrome or histamine intolerance — theoretical, not confirmed in controlled data
serious_adverse_events: No serious adverse events have been documented in peer-reviewed animal or in vitro studies at studied concentrations
Human serious adverse event data does not exist — no completed human clinical trial has been published as of mid-2026

Theoretical risk: immunomodulatory suppression of inflammatory surveillance in severely immunocompromised individuals (organ transplant recipients on calcineurin inhibitors, active HIV with low CD4 count, chemotherapy patients) — unstudied for KPV specifically
Theoretical risk: in patients with active, uncontrolled infection, blunting of the NF-kappaB inflammatory response could theoretically impair pathogen clearance — no direct evidence

synergies.rationale: BPC-157 is studied for angiogenesis, tissue regeneration, and mucosal healing (primarily via nitric oxide pathways and growth factor upregulation), while KPV is studied for suppression of NF-kappaB-driven acute and chronic inflammation. In gut-health research contexts the two are discussed together on the educational hypothesis that BPC-157 addresses structural repair while KPV modulates the inflammatory environment — complementary rather than overlapping mechanisms. Both have shown independent activity in murine intestinal injury models. No head-to-head or combination human trial exists.
synergies.caution_notes: No human combination data exists. Both are research compounds without FDA approval. Combining unvalidated agents multiplies unknown interaction risks. Clinician oversight required; this is not a dosing recommendation.

synergies.rationale: GHK-Cu (copper tripeptide-1) is studied for stimulating collagen synthesis, activating tissue-remodeling proteases, and its own NF-kappaB-modulating anti-inflammatory effects. The educational rationale discussed for co-use with KPV is that KPV may address acute inflammatory signaling while GHK-Cu may support downstream structural repair and collagen remodeling — different phases of the healing cascade. Both are tripeptides with favorable in vitro safety profiles.
synergies.caution_notes: The KLOW blend (KPV + BPC-157 + GHK-Cu + TB-500) is discussed in community research contexts only. No combination human safety or efficacy data exists. Additive or synergistic immune-modulating effects are unstudied in humans. Not a dosing recommendation.

synergies.rationale: TB-500 (related to the Ac-SDKP region of thymosin beta-4) is studied for systemic anti-inflammatory activity, cell migration, angiogenesis, and actin cytoskeletal remodeling. Discussed alongside KPV's NF-kappaB suppression on the educational hypothesis of complementary systemic (TB-500) and localized mucosal/tissue (KPV) anti-inflammatory coverage. Both appear in the community 'KLOW' research stack.
synergies.caution_notes: No human combination trial. TB-500 is also not FDA-approved. The immunomodulatory overlap of two anti-inflammatory agents in immunocompromised individuals has not been characterized. Not a dosing recommendation.

synergies.rationale: Larazotide acetate is a tight-junction regulator studied for reducing intestinal permeability via zonulin-pathway inhibition, with human celiac disease trial data (Phase 2). The educational rationale discussed for pairing with KPV is that larazotide targets the structural gut-barrier breach while KPV targets downstream inflammatory signaling — complementary targets in the barrier-to-inflammation pathway.
synergies.caution_notes: Larazotide has human Phase 2 data (celiac disease) but is not FDA-approved. KPV has no completed human trial data. The combination rationale is mechanistic only; no combination data exists and this is not a dosing recommendation.

name: GHK-Cu (Copper Tripeptide-1)
short_description: GHK-Cu is a naturally occurring tripeptide-copper(II) complex (Gly-His-Lys + Cu2+) first isolated from human plasma by Loren Pickart in 1973. It is not an FDA-approved drug. Topically it is regulated as a cosmetic ingredient (INCI: Copper Tripeptide-1) and is widely present in over-the-counter skincare. Injectable/systemic use is not FDA-approved; any compounded injectable status is subject to ongoing regulatory review. Evidence for tissue repair, wound healing, and skin remodeling derives largely from small human trials and in vitro/animal models; large-scale RCTs are absent. All content is educational and does not constitute medical advice or a prescription.
mechanism: GHK-Cu chelates copper(II) ions in a square-planar coordination complex involving the alpha-amino group of glycine, the deprotonated amide nitrogen of the Gly-His peptide bond, and the imidazole nitrogen of histidine. This configuration silences the redox activity of free Cu2+, enabling safe intracellular copper delivery. Once internalized, copper is bioavailable for copper-dependent enzymes, including superoxide dismutase (SOD1), lysyl oxidase (collagen/elastin crosslinking), and cytochrome c oxidase (mitochondrial ATP generation). At nanomolar concentrations GHK modulates expression of a large fraction of human genes (reported to affect roughly 30% of responsive genes, with both up- and down-regulation), including upregulation of collagen types I and III, elastin, glycosaminoglycans, VEGF, BDNF, and BMP-2; upregulation of ubiquitin-proteasome genes (autophagy/protein quality control); suppression of NF-kappaB pro-inflammatory signaling; and activation of the Nrf2/Keap1 antioxidant pathway. Plasma GHK declines with age (reported from roughly 200 ng/mL near age 20 to ~80 ng/mL by age 60), a basis for the proposed role in age-related tissue maintenance decline.
monitoring_notes: Screen for Wilson's disease (serum ceruloplasmin + 24-hour urine copper) in patients with unexplained liver enzyme elevation, neurological symptoms, or Kayser-Fleischer rings before initiating any copper-containing compound
Consider baseline serum copper and ceruloplasmin prior to use, especially for any injectable protocol
Consider baseline zinc level — copper and zinc share transport pathways; elevated copper may create functional zinc deficiency
Consider liver function tests (AST, ALT) at baseline in patients with hepatic risk factors
Periodic zinc:copper ratio monitoring during extended protocols per general clinical guidance

No validated clinical monitoring protocol specifically for GHK-Cu injection exists; the above derive from general copper metabolism principles and secondary clinical sources, and are educational, not a prescription
absolute_contraindications: Wilson's disease (ATP7B mutation causing impaired copper excretion — exogenous copper load can worsen hepatic and neurological copper accumulation; opposes penicillamine/trientine/zinc therapy)
Known copper allergy (e.g., prior reaction to a copper IUD)
Indian childhood cirrhosis or other hereditary hepatic copper accumulation disorders
Any condition with documented pathological copper overload
relative_contraindications: Pregnancy and breastfeeding (copper crosses the placenta; insufficient safety data for systemic administration; topical cosmetic concentrations generally considered low-risk but warrant clinician guidance)
Decompensated liver disease / cirrhosis (liver is the primary organ of copper excretion; impaired clearance may increase copper accumulation risk)
Active cancer (theoretical concern: GHK-Cu is associated with VEGF/angiogenesis and tissue remodeling — unclear implications in oncology patients; requires oncologist clearance)
Severe autoimmune conditions (immune modulation via NF-kappaB suppression may theoretically interact with immunosuppressive therapy)
Concurrent use of copper-chelating agents (penicillamine, trientine) — pharmacologically antagonistic
common_side_effects: Injection site redness, mild swelling, and transient pain (typically resolving within 24-72 hours) — most commonly reported reaction to SC injection
Mild itching at injection or application site
Transient metallic taste (reported anecdotally with systemic administration)
Blue-green discoloration of reconstituted solution (intrinsic property of the copper chelate — not an adverse event)
Localized hyperpigmentation at repeatedly injected sites (sparse community reports, attributed to local copper deposition)
serious_adverse_events: Copper toxicity (theoretical at supra-therapeutic doses; considered unlikely at community-referenced amounts given low elemental copper content, but unverified in prospective human pharmacokinetic trials)
Systemic copper accumulation in patients with undiagnosed Wilson's disease or other copper metabolism disorders (potentially serious — screen before use)
Accelerated tumor angiogenesis (theoretical — VEGF upregulation; no confirmed case reports, but a mechanistic concern in active cancer)
