name: HGH Fragment 176-191
short_description: HGH Fragment 176-191 is a synthetic 16-amino acid peptide corresponding to the C-terminal region (residues 176-191) of human growth hormone (hGH), with a tyrosine substituted at the N-terminus. Its close analog AOD9604 (Tyr-hGH177-191) has been the primary form studied in human trials. The fragment isolates the proposed lipolytic domain of hGH while theoretically avoiding the growth-promoting, IGF-1-elevating, and insulin-disrupting effects of full-length hGH. NOT FDA-approved. NOT approved by any major regulatory authority for therapeutic use. WADA-prohibited under S0 (non-approved pharmacological substances). Most human safety data derive from AOD9604; HGH Fragment 176-191 itself has no published standalone human efficacy trials. AOD9604 obesity trials did not meet their primary weight-loss endpoints and development was discontinued. For EDUCATIONAL reference only; clinician supervision required.
mechanism: HGH Fragment 176-191 contains the proposed lipolytic domain of human growth hormone. In animal models, it stimulates lipolysis via upregulation of hormone-sensitive lipase (HSL) and adipose triglyceride lipase (ATGL) through cAMP/protein kinase A signaling cascades. Chronic treatment in obese mice increases beta-3 adrenergic receptor (ADRB3) expression in adipose tissue; however, Heffernan et al. (2001) demonstrated in beta-3-AR knockout mice that ADRB3 is not the direct mediator, suggesting its upregulation contributes to enhanced downstream lipolytic sensitivity rather than being the primary trigger. Unlike full-length hGH, the fragment does not compete for the classical GH receptor, does not stimulate IGF-1 production, does not promote cell proliferation, and in animal studies does not induce hyperglycemia or reduce insulin secretion. Anti-lipogenic effects (inhibiting new fat synthesis) have also been described in preclinical models. The exact receptor or signaling target mediating its primary effect remains incompletely characterized.
monitoring_notes: Fasting blood glucose and HbA1c at baseline and periodically during use (especially in diabetic or pre-diabetic individuals)
IGF-1 level at baseline — while AOD9604 human trials showed no IGF-1 elevation, unregulated compounded forms may differ in purity and content
Lipid panel (LDL, HDL, triglycerides) at baseline and follow-up — fat mobilization may transiently alter circulating lipid fractions
Injection site inspection at each use — rotate sites to minimize local reactions
Blood pressure and resting heart rate — indirect metabolic effects on cardiovascular parameters possible with chronic lipolytic stimulation
Body composition measurement (DEXA or bioimpedance) if tracking fat mass changes over time
Screen for cancer history and hormone-sensitive conditions before any GH-axis adjacent compound use
No validated biomarker of therapeutic response for HGH Fragment 176-191 in humans has been established
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy or history of hormone-sensitive cancer (insufficient safety data; precautionary given GH-pathway adjacency)
Pregnancy (no safety data; precautionary contraindication)
Breastfeeding (no safety data; precautionary contraindication)
Known hypersensitivity to the peptide or any component of the formulation
relative_contraindications: Competitive athletes subject to WADA or USADA anti-doping rules (prohibited under S0)
Diabetes mellitus — close blood glucose monitoring warranted; lipolytic effects may interact with hypoglycemic agents
Pre-existing pituitary or hypothalamic disorders — unknown interaction with the endogenous GH axis
Pediatric populations — not studied; GH-axis effects during growth phases unknown
Concurrent use of insulin or insulin secretagogues — additive metabolic effects not characterized
Personal or family history of malignancy — insufficient long-term oncologic safety data
common_side_effects: Injection site reactions (pain, redness, swelling, pruritus) — reported in AOD9604 clinical trials at rates comparable to placebo
Headache — reported in community use, mechanism not established
Nausea — mild and transient, reported in community use
Fatigue — reported in community use
Transient dizziness — reported in community use
Flushing — occasionally reported with SC injection
serious_adverse_events: No serious adverse events were reported in the AOD9604 Phase 1/2 safety studies (Stier et al. 2013); adverse event rates were indistinguishable from placebo

Blood glucose fluctuations (transient hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia) reported in some clinical trial participants — the fragment is proposed not to affect insulin sensitivity, but individual metabolic responses may vary

Theoretical oncologic concern: use in individuals with active or prior malignancy — the fragment's effects on cancer cell biology are not fully characterized; one in vitro study (Habibullah et al. 2022, PMID 35783198) examined the fragment in a drug-delivery context for breast cancer, which does NOT imply oncologic safety in a body-recomposition context

Theoretical immunogenicity concern: no anti-AOD9604 antibodies were detected in clinical trial participants (Stier et al. 2013), but long-term immunologic effects of unregulated compounded forms are unknown
WADA S0 violation risk for competitive athletes — AOD9604 and HGH Fragment 176-191 are prohibited in sport under the S0 category (non-approved pharmacological substances)

This row is provided solely as an educational reference to the range most commonly cited in the research community. It does NOT constitute a clinical recommendation or dosing guidance. Users should discuss any use with a licensed clinician.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanistic context: CJC-1295 is a GHRH analog that increases pulsatile GH release from the pituitary (upstream), while HGH Fragment 176-191 is proposed to act downstream directly on adipose lipolysis. The two are sometimes discussed together in the research community on the theory that upstream GH-pulse modulation and direct peripheral lipolytic signaling target different points in the pathway. No human RCT has evaluated this combination; evidence is mechanistic and anecdotal only.
synergies.caution_notes: CJC-1295 increases GH and IGF-1; pairing a GH secretagogue with a GH fragment raises a theoretical concern of cumulative GH-axis stimulation. Individuals with personal or family history of hormone-sensitive tumors should avoid GH-axis peptide combinations. Neither compound is FDA-approved. This is not a recommendation to combine them.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanistic context: Ipamorelin is a selective ghrelin-receptor agonist (GHRP) associated with short-burst GH release and minimal cortisol or prolactin elevation. It is sometimes discussed alongside HGH Fragment 176-191 because they act at different points (pituitary GH pulsatility vs. direct adipose lipolysis). The pairing appears frequently in body-composition discussions in the research literature but has no human trial data.
synergies.caution_notes: Ipamorelin elevates GH and modestly IGF-1. Co-use with a lipolytic GH fragment has not been evaluated for additive safety signals in humans. Not FDA-approved. Caution in any patient with a history of malignancy or active pituitary pathology. This is not a recommendation to combine them.

synergies.rationale: Educational note: AOD-9604 (Tyr-hGH177-191) is a modified form closely related to HGH Fragment 176-191 and is essentially the same compound studied in human AOD9604 trials. Some sources distinguish the unmodified fragment from the N-terminally Tyr-modified form and suggest AOD9604 may have different oral bioavailability/stability. Human safety data for one largely inform understanding of the other; they are not complementary agents.
synergies.caution_notes: Human AOD9604 obesity trials did not demonstrate significant weight loss vs placebo. GRAS status applies to oral food-ingredient use only, not pharmaceutical dosing. WADA-prohibited. Because the two forms are essentially the same compound, co-use would be redundant and is not supported by evidence.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanistic context: BPC-157 (Body Protection Compound 157) is discussed alongside HGH Fragment 176-191 in some body-recomposition research contexts because the two are proposed to address different targets (connective tissue/gut repair signaling vs. adipose metabolism). The pairing is mechanistically plausible as non-overlapping but has no human trial data evaluating it directly.
synergies.caution_notes: BPC-157 is also a research-only, non-FDA-approved peptide. Neither compound has established human efficacy data, and the combination has no clinical trial safety evaluation. Educational context only; not a recommendation to combine them.

name: CJC-1295 Without DAC (Mod GRF 1-29)
short_description: Mod GRF 1-29 is a synthetic 29-amino-acid analog of endogenous growth hormone-releasing hormone (GHRH 1-29) with four amino acid substitutions that improve enzymatic stability and receptor binding affinity. It is NOT FDA-approved; the structurally distinct GHRH analog tesamorelin (Egrifta) holds the only FDA approval in this class. Mod GRF 1-29 is research-only and is explicitly prohibited by WADA (Category S2) and the U.S. Department of Defense. No prospective human trial has established a therapeutic or safe dose for Mod GRF 1-29 itself; the published human pharmacology in this family was conducted with CJC-1295 WITH DAC, a distinct long-acting compound.
mechanism: Mod GRF 1-29 binds to growth hormone-releasing hormone receptors (GHRHR) on anterior pituitary somatotroph cells, stimulating synthesis and pulsatile release of endogenous growth hormone (GH). Four amino acid substitutions relative to native GRF 1-29 (sermorelin) are responsible for its improved pharmacokinetic profile: D-Ala at position 2 (resists cleavage by dipeptidyl peptidase-4), Gln at position 8 (resists asparagine deamidation), Ala at position 15, and Leu at position 27 (resists methionine oxidation). Unlike sermorelin (unmodified GRF 1-29, half-life ~5 minutes), Mod GRF 1-29 has a reported half-life of approximately 30 minutes, producing a defined GH pulse that clears before the next dose, which in principle allows somatostatin feedback to reset and preserves pituitary sensitivity. Downstream GH release triggers hepatic production of IGF-1, which mediates anabolic, lipolytic, and tissue-repair effects. The compound does not bind albumin (no Drug Affinity Complex), distinguishing it from CJC-1295 with DAC, which has a half-life of 6-8 days.
monitoring_notes: Baseline IGF-1 before initiating any GH-axis protocol; repeat at 4-6 weeks and every 3 months; aim for upper-normal for age, not supraphysiologic
Fasting glucose and HbA1c at baseline and every 3 months — GH is insulin-antagonistic; risk elevated in pre-diabetes and T2D
Thyroid panel (TSH, free T4) at baseline — GH stimulation increases peripheral T4-to-T3 conversion; hypothyroidism may be unmasked
Monitor for signs of fluid retention: weight, blood pressure, ankle edema, carpal tunnel symptoms
Periodic complete metabolic panel for hepatic and renal function
Monitor for injection site reactions; rotate sites
Symptom surveillance for headache, visual changes, or signs of increased intracranial pressure

Pregnancy — effects on fetal development unstudied; GH-axis stimulation is contraindicated

relative_contraindications: Type 2 diabetes or insulin resistance — GH elevates blood glucose via insulin antagonism; close glucose monitoring warranted; particular caution in individuals already on insulin or oral hypoglycemics

Fluid retention states (heart failure, nephrotic syndrome, hepatic cirrhosis) — GH-class sodium and water retention may worsen these conditions

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanistic rationale: Mod GRF 1-29 acts on GHRH receptors (GHRHR) to stimulate GH synthesis and prime somatotrophs, while ipamorelin acts on ghrelin receptors (GHSR-1a) to amplify pulsatile GH release and oppose somatostatin — complementary, non-competing pathways. Human evidence for GHRH + GHRP synergy comes from Veldhuis and Bowers (Am J Physiol Endocrinol Metab 2009; PMID 19240251), which used GHRP-2 (not ipamorelin) and showed combined GH responses exceeding either agent alone. Ipamorelin is frequently described as more selective than older GHRPs because it raises GH with comparatively little effect on cortisol or prolactin (Raun et al., Eur J Endocrinol 1998; PMID 9849822). The specific Mod GRF 1-29 + ipamorelin pairing has not been directly tested in a human RCT.
synergies.caution_notes: Combining a GHRH analog with a GHRP amplifies GH and IGF-1 elevation beyond either agent alone, which can magnify GH-class adverse effects (fluid retention, insulin resistance, carpal-tunnel symptoms, IGF-1-dependent growth promotion). The supporting human synergy data used GHRP-2 with a GHRH analog, not ipamorelin with Mod GRF 1-29; this exact pairing is not RCT-validated. IGF-1 and glucose monitoring are appropriate. Educational context only; discuss with a clinician.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanistic rationale: GHRP-2 is the GHRP used in the Veldhuis/Bowers 2009 human RCT (PMID 19240251) that demonstrated GHRH + GHRP synergy in men across the lifespan, so the synergy concept itself has direct human support. GHRP-2 is more potent per unit than ipamorelin but is also reported to raise cortisol, prolactin, and ACTH to a greater degree, which is often described as a disadvantage relative to ipamorelin.
synergies.caution_notes: GHRP-2 is associated with dose-dependent cortisol and prolactin elevation, unlike ipamorelin's more selective profile. Pairing with Mod GRF 1-29 amplifies both GH release and off-target hormonal effects. The human RCT supporting synergy used a GHRH analog generically, not Mod GRF 1-29 specifically. Educational context only; discuss with a clinician.

synergies.rationale: Educational mechanistic rationale only: BPC-157 is a tissue-repair peptide with GH receptor-modulating properties reported in animal models. The community stacking rationale is complementary mechanisms — Mod GRF 1-29 drives systemic GH/IGF-1 elevation while BPC-157 is associated with local tissue repair and GI integrity in animal studies. No human RCT has examined this combination; the mechanistic basis is animal data only.
synergies.caution_notes: No human clinical data on this combination. Both compounds are research-only and not FDA-approved. Combined IGF-1 effects are unstudied. Educational reference only; discuss with a clinician.

name: P21 (P021)
short_description: P21 (also designated P021) is a synthetic, adamantane-modified tetrapeptide (Ac-DGGLAG-NH2) derived from residues 148-151 of ciliary neurotrophic factor (CNTF). It is a research-only compound — NOT FDA-approved and NOT evaluated in human clinical trials. All published efficacy and safety data originate exclusively from rodent models. It is studied for potential neurogenesis promotion, neuroprotection, and cognitive support in Alzheimer's disease and traumatic brain injury animal models. It should not be confused with the tumor-suppressor protein p21/CDKN1A.
mechanism: P21 operates through two complementary pathways documented in animal studies. First, it inhibits leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF) signaling, which normally suppresses adult neurogenesis; by blocking LIF, it disinhibits hippocampal dentate gyrus neurogenesis and increases proliferation of neural progenitor cells. Second, it upregulates brain-derived neurotrophic factor (BDNF) expression at the transcriptional level; elevated BDNF activates the TrkB receptor, which through the PI3K/AKT pathway leads to inhibitory phosphorylation of GSK3-beta (Ser9), thereby reducing tau hyperphosphorylation and amyloid-beta accumulation. An adamantylated glycine appended to the C-terminus improves blood-brain barrier permeability and resistance to exopeptidase degradation relative to native CNTF. The compound avoids CNTF's direct receptor agonism, which previously caused dose-limiting anorexia and weight loss in human CNTF trials (Axokine program), although whether this advantage translates to humans is entirely unknown.
monitoring_notes: There are no established human monitoring protocols for P21 — the following are precautionary educational suggestions only
Clinician discussion required before any self-administration given complete absence of human pharmacokinetic and safety data
Baseline cognitive assessment (if used in a research context) to detect any adverse neurological changes
Baseline and periodic neurological symptom review
Liver function tests as a general precaution for any novel peptide administered chronically (no specific hepatotoxicity signal, but also no human data)
Any new neurological symptoms (seizures, headaches, cognitive changes, mood disturbance) warrant immediate cessation and clinical evaluation
This compound is NOT approved for human use and should only be handled as a research chemical in appropriate research settings
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy or history of CNS neoplasm (precautionary — CNTF pathway involvement in glioma biology; no specific P21 data)
Pregnancy and breastfeeding (no safety data in any reproductive model; absolute precautionary contraindication)
Use in individuals under 25 years of age (brain development still ongoing; neurogenic pathway manipulation is unstudied in developing human brains)
relative_contraindications: Personal or strong family history of non-CNS cancer (theoretical proliferative concern; unquantified risk)
Seizure disorder or epilepsy (neurogenesis and excitability changes are mechanistically plausible concerns; not studied)
Autoimmune or neuroinflammatory conditions (CNTF pathway interactions with immune signaling are complex and unstudied in this context)
Concurrent use of other neurotrophin-modulating agents (additive BDNF effects uncharacterized)
common_side_effects: No human adverse event data exists — all safety observations are from rodent studies
In animal studies (up to 18 months): no anorexia, no hyperalgesia, no significant weight loss, no observable toxicity reported
Unlike parent molecule CNTF (which caused clinically significant anorexia and weight loss in the Axokine human trials), P21 did not reproduce these effects in rodent models
Potential for antibody formation against P21 has been raised theoretically; no immune reaction to P21 has been observed in animal studies to date
Injection-site reactions are possible with subcutaneous administration (extrapolated from general peptide administration practice; not specifically studied for P21)
serious_adverse_events: No serious adverse events have been documented in preclinical animal studies
CRITICAL: No human safety data exists at any dose or route — the absence of observed rodent toxicity does NOT establish human safety

CNTF receptor signaling is implicated in certain glioma subtypes (CNTFRα overexpressed in lower-grade gliomas per PMC5539284 and PMC4762039) — the relevance of this finding to P21's modified mechanism is unknown and unstudied

Theoretical concern: compounds promoting cellular proliferation (neurogenesis) carry an uncharacterized theoretical risk in individuals with occult or active neoplasms; this is a class-level precautionary flag, not a documented P21-specific signal
Long-term effects on the human brain's endogenous neurogenic regulation are completely unknown

synergies.rationale: Both compounds upregulate BDNF but through distinct upstream mechanisms: P21 acts via LIF inhibition and CNTF pathway modulation, while Semax acts through ACTH/melanocortin receptor pathways affecting serotonergic and dopaminergic systems. In community research-practice discussion, this complementarity is framed educationally as acute cognitive optimization (Semax) plus longer-term structural neurogenesis support (P21). There are no co-administration animal studies or human data for this combination.

No safety data exists for any human co-administration. Educational framing only; not a protocol or dosing recommendation.

synergies.rationale: Dihexa is a hepatocyte growth factor (HGF)/c-Met potentiator studied for synaptogenesis in animal models. Community rationale for discussing it alongside P21 is that Dihexa is associated with structural synaptogenesis via HGF/c-Met while P21 is associated with BDNF-mediated neurogenesis via LIF inhibition — two non-overlapping neuroplasticity pathways. No co-administration studies exist in any species.
synergies.caution_notes: Neither P21 nor Dihexa has human clinical trial data. Dihexa has its own uncharacterized risk profile. Any combination is entirely speculative and carries unknown interaction risks. Educational reference only; not a protocol or dosing recommendation.

synergies.rationale: BPC-157 is studied for broad neuroprotective and tissue-repair effects through nitric oxide and growth factor pathways. Community rationale for discussing it alongside P21 is complementary neuroprotection: P21 is associated with BDNF-neurogenesis and tau pathology, while BPC-157 is associated with vascular and growth factor repair mechanisms — described as non-overlapping mechanistic targets.
synergies.caution_notes: No co-administration studies exist. Both are research-only compounds without human clinical validation. Educational reference only; not a protocol or dosing recommendation.

name: Pentadeca Arginate (PDA)
short_description: Pentadeca Arginate (PDA) is the arginate salt form of the 15-amino-acid synthetic peptide BPC-157 (sequence: GEPPPGKPADDAGLV). The vast majority of supporting preclinical evidence derives from BPC-157 acetate research — no peer-reviewed study has investigated PDA as a distinct molecular entity. PDA is NOT FDA-approved for any indication; placement of BPC-157 acetate on the FDA Category 2 bulk substances list is not an approval. It is an experimental, research-only compound and is WADA-prohibited under S0.
mechanism: PDA shares the proposed mechanism of BPC-157: a pleiotropic synthetic pentadecapeptide that modulates the nitric oxide (NO) system via eNOS/Akt signaling, activates VEGFR2 to promote angiogenesis and neovascularization, upregulates early-growth-response genes (Egr-1, Akt1, Nos3, Vegfa) within minutes of exposure, stimulates collagen synthesis and fibroblast proliferation, suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-1beta, IL-6), acts as a free-radical scavenger via carboxylic groups, and modulates dopaminergic and serotonergic neurotransmitter systems. It also activates ERK1/2 signaling relevant to epithelial and musculoskeletal repair. These mechanisms are established in animal models (predominantly rats) and in-vitro systems. Translation to humans is not validated. The arginate salt form does not alter the active peptide sequence; any mechanistic differences from the acetate form are speculative and unstudied.
monitoring_notes: Baseline and periodic liver function tests (AST, ALT, ALP) if used long-term — no established monitoring interval exists; prudent given unknown metabolic profile
Monitor injection sites for signs of infection, abscess, or persistent inflammation with subcutaneous administration
Clinician review before initiating if any personal or family cancer history — angiogenic risk assessment warranted
No established therapeutic drug monitoring assay exists for BPC-157 or PDA in humans
Regulatory status is in flux (FDA Category 2 enforcement; evolving compounding policy). Verify current legal compounding status with a clinician/pharmacist before any use.
WADA/anti-doping: athletes should confirm their status before any use; no therapeutic use exemption pathway exists
Document all use as user-supplied / clinician-supervised experimental protocol; not standard of care
absolute_contraindications: Active malignancy (any cancer): angiogenic mechanism represents a theoretical risk of tumor vascularization and accelerated growth
Pregnancy: complete absence of safety data; angiogenic and growth-promoting properties pose unknown fetal risk
Breastfeeding: no pharmacokinetic data on breast milk transfer; unknown infant exposure risk
Known hypersensitivity to BPC-157, PDA, or any component of the formulation
relative_contraindications: Personal or strong family history of cancer — discuss risk-benefit with an oncology-informed clinician before any use
Benign tumors or conditions with angiogenesis-dependent growth (e.g., uterine fibroids, endometriosis, hemangiomas)
Concurrent use of anticoagulants — BPC-157 may affect platelet function and vascular tone; interaction profile unstudied in humans
Concurrent psychotropic medications (SSRIs, dopaminergics, GABAergics) — BPC-157 modulates dopamine and serotonin systems; no human interaction data
Immunosuppressed patients or those on immunomodulatory therapy — unknown immune interaction profile
Competitive athletes subject to WADA testing — prohibited under WADA S0 (Unapproved Substances); risks an automatic anti-doping rule violation
Pediatric populations — no safety data whatsoever
common_side_effects: Injection site reactions (localized redness, swelling, discomfort) — reported anecdotally in community use
Nausea — reported anecdotally at higher doses in community use
Dizziness or lightheadedness — rare, anecdotal
Fatigue — rare, anecdotal
Note: No formal adverse-event database exists for PDA or BPC-157 in humans; the above reflects anecdotal community reports, not controlled clinical observation
serious_adverse_events: Theoretical pro-oncogenic risk: BPC-157 promotes angiogenesis via VEGF/VEGFR2 upregulation — the same pathway exploited by tumors for neovascularization and metastasis. No human cancer cases attributable to BPC-157 have been published, but the mechanistic concern is unresolved. Per PMC12567171 (2025 reply): 'no published in vivo data demonstrate that BPC 157 inhibits tumor progression, reduces tumor volume, or suppresses metastasis.'

Nitric oxide system dysregulation: Independent research shows BPC-157 disrupts the Cav-1/eNOS inhibitory complex and promotes NO production dose-dependently. Excessive NO at supratherapeutic doses may inhibit cytochrome P450 metabolism, impair hemoglobin function, or generate peroxynitrite — a reactive nitrogen species linked to neurodegeneration and DNA damage.
Unknown long-term immunogenicity: FDA cited concerns about immune reactions and peptide impurities in compounded formulations when placing BPC-157 on the Category 2 list in 2023.
Absence of long-term safety data: USADA states no one knows if there is a safe dose, or any way to use this compound safely, in humans. No multi-year human exposure data exists.

Pharmacokinetic uncertainty at human exposures: Most animal studies use 10 ng/kg to 10 mcg/kg, while community practice cites 250-500 mcg flat doses (substantially higher on a mcg/kg basis for a typical adult). Per PMC12567171, 'there is no crucial information on what occurs with higher, repeated, or long-term exposures.'

This is the only controlled human-exposure reference for the peptide sequence; it does not apply to the arginate form (PDA) and cannot be used to derive a human therapeutic dose. The cited reference is a rat study that mentions the PL-14736 clinical program; no standalone human trial publication exists.

dose_references.notes: A daily subcutaneous dose of 250-500 mcg is a widely cited reference range for BPC-157 / PDA in adults in off-label contexts. This is a community/research-practice convention, not clinically validated: it is NOT derived from any peer-reviewed human clinical trial, is NOT FDA-validated, and carries no established human safety or efficacy evidence. Cited here solely as a commonly-documented reference range for educational purposes and must be presented with a clinician disclaimer. Not a recommendation.

synergies.rationale: BPC-157/PDA and TB-500 are proposed to act via complementary, non-overlapping pathways: BPC-157/PDA promotes local angiogenesis and collagen deposition at injury sites via VEGFR2/eNOS/ERK1/2 signaling, while TB-500 promotes cell migration, actin polymerization, and anti-inflammatory signaling via G-actin sequestration. A combined effect on musculoskeletal tissue repair is mechanistically plausible. No head-to-head or combination study in humans exists; all supporting data are preclinical animal studies or mechanistic reasoning.
synergies.caution_notes: No human combination safety data. Both are WADA-prohibited. Additive angiogenic stimulation is a theoretical concern in individuals with occult or active malignancy. Educational framing only; not a protocol or recommendation.

synergies.rationale: GHK-Cu is a copper-binding tripeptide that independently upregulates collagen and elastin synthesis, reduces oxidative stress via antioxidant enzyme induction, and modulates TGF-beta signaling relevant to tissue remodeling. Its collagen-modulatory mechanism is mechanistically complementary to PDA's angiogenic and fibroblast-activating actions. No prospective human combination trial exists.
synergies.caution_notes: Combination not studied in any controlled human or animal trial. Claims of synergy are mechanistically plausible but unvalidated. Educational context only; not a recommendation.

synergies.rationale: KPV is an alpha-MSH-derived anti-inflammatory tripeptide that reduces NF-kB signaling and pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-1beta, IL-6) in gut epithelium. Combined with BPC-157/PDA's proposed gut cytoprotective and angiogenic properties, a complementary anti-inflammatory and structural-repair rationale exists for gastrointestinal contexts. No peer-reviewed animal or human combination study has been published.
synergies.caution_notes: No human or animal combination data. Mechanistic rationale only. The 'KLOW' blend (GHK-Cu + BPC-157/PDA + TB-500 + KPV) appears in compounding practice but lacks any formal clinical evaluation as a combination. Educational context only.

synergies.rationale: GLP-1-axis agonists frequently cause gastrointestinal side effects (nausea, vomiting, gastroparesis-like symptoms). BPC-157/PDA's proposed gut cytoprotective, mucosal-repair, and anti-inflammatory properties have prompted interest in whether they might affect GI tolerability during GLP-1 therapy. Rapid caloric-restriction-associated lean-mass loss during GLP-1 therapy has also driven interest in tissue-repair peptides. This rationale is mechanistic and anecdotal only — no clinical study has evaluated this combination.
synergies.caution_notes: Purely mechanistic rationale. No pharmacokinetic or pharmacodynamic interaction data exist. Combined angiogenic stimulation from GLP-1 vascular effects and BPC-157 VEGF upregulation has not been characterized. Discuss any combination with a qualified clinician. Educational framing only; not a recommendation.

name: Thymalin
short_description: Thymalin is a polypeptide complex extracted from bovine (calf) thymus glands, developed in the 1970s-1980s by Russian researchers Khavinson and Morozov. It is NOT a single synthetic peptide but a heterogeneous mixture of low-molecular-weight peptides (below 10 kDa), including the active dipeptides Lys-Glu (KE, also marketed as Vilon) and Glu-Trp (EW, also marketed as Thymogen). Approved as a pharmaceutical drug in Russia since 1982 for immunodeficiency states, it is NOT FDA-approved and remains a research compound in the United States. Its primary interest in longevity research stems from a non-blinded Russian observational cohort study suggesting mortality reduction in elderly subjects. Evidence quality is HUMAN_OBS (non-randomized, non-blinded, single research group), and independent Western replication is absent.
mechanism: Thymalin is proposed to restore thymic immune function that declines with age (thymic involution). Its active dipeptides modulate hematopoietic stem cell differentiation toward mature T-lymphocytes: in vitro data show reduced CD44 and CD117 expression (stem cell/intermediate markers) by 2-3-fold and increased CD28 expression (mature T-lymphocyte marker) by 6.8-fold. The KE dipeptide activates macrophages, lymphocytes, thymocytes, and neutrophils, and induces CD4/CD5 expression on thymic cells, promoting T-helper differentiation. The EW dipeptide inhibits ACE2 expression and reduces angiotensin-induced vasoconstriction. Both dipeptides bind specific DNA promoter sequences (GCGC and GGAG respectively) to modulate gene expression governing immune response and inflammation. In vitro, the combination reduces pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-alpha (5-6-fold), IL-6 (2.5-fold), and IL-1beta (2.7-fold). The net proposed effect is restoration of CD4+/CD8+ ratios, enhanced NK cell activity, and improved adaptive immune competence in immunosenescent individuals. Mechanistic findings are largely in vitro and from a single research lineage.
monitoring_notes: Baseline immune panel before initiating: CBC with differential, CD3/CD4/CD8 T-cell subsets, CD4:CD8 ratio, NK cell counts, immunoglobulins (IgG, IgA, IgM)
Repeat immune panel 4-6 weeks after completing a course to assess T-cell response
Monitor for injection site reactions at each administration
Screen for autoimmune markers at baseline (ANA, anti-dsDNA, thyroid antibodies) given theoretical autoimmune risk with immunostimulation
Clinical assessment for signs of hypersensitivity (urticaria, angioedema, systemic allergy), particularly with first 1-3 doses given bovine protein origin
Clinician oversight required: not for self-administration without medical supervision
Source verification: confirm bovine material is from BSE-free herds with documented prion-reduction processing
absolute_contraindications: Known hypersensitivity or allergy to bovine-derived proteins or thymic extracts
Active solid organ transplant recipients requiring immunosuppression (immunostimulation may theoretically precipitate rejection)
relative_contraindications: Active autoimmune diseases (e.g., lupus, rheumatoid arthritis, multiple sclerosis, Hashimoto's thyroiditis, type 1 diabetes): non-specific T-cell upregulation may theoretically worsen autoimmune activity; risk-benefit requires specialist evaluation
Active hematologic malignancies (e.g., chronic lymphocytic leukemia): immune modulation in lymphoproliferative disease requires oncologist clearance
Concurrent immunosuppressive therapy (relative: pharmacodynamic antagonism)
Pregnancy and lactation: no safety data available
Pediatric use: no safety data available in children

Bovine prion disease risk (BSE/CJD): bovine-derived polypeptide extracts carry theoretical transmissible spongiform encephalopathy risk; manufacturer sourcing and prion-reduction steps should be verified
common_side_effects: Injection site pain, redness, or swelling (most commonly reported adverse effect, typically resolving within 24-48 hours)
Mild local inflammatory reaction at intramuscular injection site
Possible allergic or hypersensitivity reaction to bovine-derived protein components
serious_adverse_events: Hypersensitivity or anaphylactic reaction to bovine thymus-derived polypeptides (theoretical; not quantified in available literature)
Theoretical exacerbation of autoimmune disease due to non-specific immune upregulation (mechanism-based concern; not documented in available controlled data)
Unknown long-term safety profile outside the Russian pharmacovigilance system; independent Western safety surveillance is absent
Potential immunostimulatory risk in organ transplant recipients on immunosuppression (theoretical; graft-rejection concern)

synergies.rationale: The most studied pairing in Khavinson's research program. Epitalon is a pineal peptide bioregulator (tetrapeptide Ala-Glu-Asp-Gly) studied alongside Thymalin via a proposed pineal-thymic axis: Thymalin is associated with thymic immune output while Epitalon is associated with pineal melatonin regulation and telomerase activity. The 2003 observational cohort reported greater mortality reduction with the combination than with either agent alone. Educational rationale only; the data come from a single non-blinded research group and do not establish a validated combined benefit.
synergies.caution_notes: Evidence is from a single non-blinded, non-randomized Russian cohort. No independent RCT confirms additive or synergistic mortality benefit. Both compounds are research-only (not FDA-approved). Educational/tracking framing only; not a clinical recommendation or instruction to combine.

synergies.rationale: Thymosin Alpha-1 is a synthetic 28-amino-acid thymic peptide with overlapping immunomodulatory mechanisms: both are associated with T-cell differentiation and function, and they target arguably complementary pathways within thymic immune signaling (Ta1 is associated with dendritic cell activation and Th1 responses via Toll-like receptor pathways). Ta1 has substantially stronger clinical evidence (multiple RCTs in hepatitis B, sepsis, and COVID-19). This pairing is discussed mechanistically in the literature; no human trial has studied the specific combination.
synergies.caution_notes: No human trial has evaluated Thymalin + Ta1 in combination. Theoretical immunostimulatory overlap only. In autoimmune conditions, combined immune upregulation carries a theoretical risk of disease flare. Educational/tracking framing only; not a recommendation to combine.

synergies.rationale: GHK-Cu (Gly-His-Lys copper chelate) is associated in laboratory work with modulating expression of a large number of genes toward a younger expression pattern, including anti-inflammatory and tissue-repair gene sets. Thymalin is associated with immune-senescence mechanisms via T-cell pathways; GHK-Cu is associated with broader cellular gene-expression mechanisms. These are sometimes discussed as conceptually complementary in longevity contexts. Rationale is mechanistic/theoretical; no human trial has evaluated this combination.
synergies.caution_notes: No human trial exists for this combination. Rationale is theoretical, drawn from separate mechanistic lines of evidence. GHK-Cu human data is also limited. Educational/tracking framing only; not a clinical protocol or recommendation to combine.

name: Kisspeptin
short_description: Kisspeptin is a family of endogenous neuropeptides (most studied isoforms: KP-10 and KP-54) encoded by the KISS1 gene. As the master upstream regulator of the hypothalamic-pituitary-gonadal (HPG) axis, kisspeptin stimulates GnRH pulsatility, triggering downstream LH, FSH, and sex steroid (testosterone/estradiol) release. It is NOT FDA-approved; in October 2024, the FDA Pharmacy Compounding Advisory Committee (PCAC) voted unanimously against including kisspeptin-10 on the 503A compounding bulks list. Human RCT evidence exists for IVF oocyte maturation triggering and hypoactive sexual desire disorder. Off-label use (subcutaneous KP-10) circulates in the research/biohacking community for testosterone support, but this is NOT clinically validated. Pulsatile dosing is critical — continuous administration causes rapid GPR54 receptor desensitization (tachyphylaxis). This entry is educational and does not constitute a dosing recommendation; consult a qualified clinician.
mechanism: Kisspeptin peptides (KP-10, KP-54, and intermediate isoforms) are proteolytic cleavage products of the 145-amino acid KISS1 gene product. They bind the GPR54 receptor (also called KISS1R), a Gq/11-coupled GPCR, on GnRH neurons in the hypothalamic arcuate nucleus and preoptic area. GPR54 activation triggers phospholipase C-mediated calcium signaling, depolarizing GnRH neurons and stimulating pulsatile GnRH secretion. GnRH then drives pituitary release of LH and FSH, which in turn stimulate gonadal testosterone (men) and estradiol/progesterone (women) production. Arcuate kisspeptin neurons (co-expressing NKB and dynorphin — the KNDy neurons) govern pulsatile GnRH, while preoptic area kisspeptin neurons trigger the mid-cycle LH surge in women. Beyond the HPG axis, KISS1R is expressed in adipose tissue, brown adipose tissue, liver, and pancreas, suggesting roles in energy expenditure, glucose homeostasis, and thermogenesis — though human evidence for these peripheral effects is sparse. KISS1 also functions as a metastasis suppressor gene in some tumor types, though the therapeutic relevance is distinct from the hormonal peptide effects and highly context-dependent.
monitoring_notes: Baseline hormone panel before initiating: serum LH, FSH, total testosterone (men) or estradiol (women), SHBG, prolactin
Repeat LH and FSH at 2-4 weeks to assess HPG axis engagement; diminishing response suggests GPR54 desensitization (tachyphylaxis)
Monitor total and free testosterone (men) or estradiol/progesterone (women) for supraphysiological elevation
Hematocrit/CBC if testosterone elevation is sustained — polycythemia risk
PSA in men over 40 or those with prostate history, as testosterone elevation may affect PSA levels
Cycle kisspeptin use with adequate off-periods (community convention: 30 days on / 30 days off, not clinically validated) to allow GPR54 receptor recovery
In IVF context: ultrasound follicular monitoring and estradiol levels per standard IVF protocol
No established therapeutic drug monitoring assay for kisspeptin in clinical practice
All monitoring should be directed by a qualified clinician
absolute_contraindications: Active hormone-sensitive cancers (breast cancer, prostate cancer, endometrial cancer, ovarian cancer) — acute sex steroid stimulation could accelerate tumor growth
Known hypersensitivity to kisspeptin or any excipient in formulation
Pregnancy (exogenous administration has not been established as safe; high endogenous kisspeptin levels exist naturally in normal pregnancy)
Precocious puberty or conditions where sex steroid elevation is contraindicated
relative_contraindications: History of hormone-sensitive cancer in remission — elevated sex steroids may theoretically increase recurrence risk
Polycythemia or elevated hematocrit — testosterone-stimulating compounds can worsen erythrocytosis
Conditions exacerbated by estradiol elevation (e.g., endometriosis, uterine fibroids) in women
Active use of GnRH agonists or antagonists — pharmacodynamic conflict at the HPG axis level
Concurrent testosterone replacement therapy — kisspeptin-driven LH stimulation may have reduced effect in the setting of exogenous androgen feedback suppression
Unknown reproductive-tract pathology — HPG axis stimulation without evaluation of underlying cause may mask or complicate diagnosis
Children and adolescents — pubertal timing implications are unknown with exogenous kisspeptin
common_side_effects: Mild flushing or warmth (brief, transient)
Mild injection-site redness or discomfort (SC route)
Occasional mild headache
Mild nausea (rare)
Transient elevations in LH, FSH, and sex steroids (pharmacodynamic effect)
GPR54 receptor desensitization / tachyphylaxis with repeated or continuous dosing (documented in human studies)
serious_adverse_events: Supraphysiological sex steroid elevation (testosterone, estradiol) — risk with high or repeated doses; cardiovascular implications not well studied
Exacerbation of hormone-sensitive cancers (breast, prostate, endometrial, ovarian) due to acute sex steroid stimulation — theoretical but a primary contraindication concern
Ovarian hyperstimulation syndrome (OHSS) in IVF context — risk is substantially lower vs. hCG trigger but mild cases were observed in trials
Unknown long-term safety: no long-term human safety data on repeated kisspeptin administration outside of the IVF window; chronic HPG axis effects unstudied
FDA PCAC October 2024 cited insufficient human safety data as a reason for voting against compounding inclusion

dose_references.notes: This range reflects community/research-practice convention, not clinically validated. It is documented across peptide-community sources (e.g., thepeptidecatalog.com, peptidewiki.co), is NOT derived from peer-reviewed RCT data, and does NOT constitute a medical recommendation. The FDA PCAC voted unanimously in October 2024 AGAINST including kisspeptin-10 on the 503A compounding bulks list. No human RCT has validated this specific dose range, route, or cycle pattern. Tachyphylaxis risk is real and clinically documented with chronic dosing. Educational tracking reference only.

synergies.rationale: Kisspeptin acts upstream of GnRH neurons; gonadorelin is exogenous GnRH acting at the pituitary level. Mechanistically, kisspeptin influences hypothalamic GnRH neuron activity while gonadorelin acts directly at the pituitary — two different tiers of the same axis. This is educational mechanism only; the two represent redundant HPG-axis stimulation and the combination is not a validated protocol.
synergies.caution_notes: No clinical RCT data on this combination exists. Dual-axis stimulation could cause supraphysiological LH/FSH surges and combined receptor desensitization at both GPR54 and GnRH receptors. Educational framing only — not a recommendation. Discuss any combination with a qualified clinician.

synergies.rationale: Enclomiphene is a selective estrogen receptor modulator (SERM) that blocks estrogen negative feedback at the hypothalamus and pituitary. Mechanistically these two compounds act at different points of the HPG feedback loop: kisspeptin relates to upstream GnRH neuron firing; enclomiphene relates to removal of downstream estrogen-mediated inhibition. This mechanistic rationale has been discussed in research-community literature for secondary hypogonadism but is not a validated combined protocol.
synergies.caution_notes: No peer-reviewed clinical trial has validated this combination. Both compounds affect the HPG axis; combined use could produce unpredictable LH/FSH surges, estradiol fluctuations, or exacerbate conditions sensitive to sex-steroid changes. Community protocols combining these are anecdotal. Educational only; clinician oversight required.

synergies.rationale: In male hypogonadism and fertility contexts, hCG acts as an LH analog stimulating testicular Leydig cells directly, while kisspeptin acts upstream relating to endogenous LH pulsatility. The contrast between a direct LH-receptor agonist and an upstream HPG modulator is described in the literature, including IVF settings where kisspeptin is studied as an oocyte-maturation trigger. This is educational mechanism only — no established combined clinical protocol exists.
synergies.caution_notes: hCG is FDA-approved for specific indications (cryptorchidism, hypogonadotropic hypogonadism, ovulation induction), but combination use with kisspeptin has no established clinical protocol. Risk of supraphysiological testosterone/estradiol, exacerbation of hormone-sensitive conditions, and polycythemia. Educational framing only; clinician supervision required.
